title: ODM upstream: true ortho as optional post-processing step
description:
After validating the true ortho approach in geo-51u (Python post-processing tool), contribute it upstream to ODM as an optional pipeline step.

**Two possible integration paths:**

1. **Post-processing step (lower friction):** Add a new ODM stage (e.g. odm_true_ortho) that runs after odm_orthophoto. Requires --dtm (needs both DSM and DTM). Produces true_orthophoto.tif alongside the standard ortho. Opt-in via --true-ortho flag.

2. **Integrated into odm_orthophoto (higher impact):** Modify the existing orthophoto generator to do visibility-aware camera selection when DTM is available. More invasive change but eliminates the duplicate ortho.

Path 1 is more likely to be accepted upstream — it's additive, doesn't change existing behavior, and can be pure Python.

**Prerequisites:**
- geo-51u validated on aztec6 (before/after comparison via rmse_visual_report)
- Performance benchmarked (time + memory on aztec-scale job)
- Clean Python implementation suitable for upstream contribution

**Depends on:** geo-51u (validate the approach first)

title: Restyle zoom/standard view toggle to match Filter By Distance button
description:
Two problems with the current zoom/standard view toggle button:

1. Invisible on standard view: button has white background and blends into the
   white toolbar — only visible on hover.

2. Confusing label: when in zoom view it says 'Standard View', and when in
   standard view it says 'Zoom View' (i.e. it labels the OTHER view, not the
   current state). This is the opposite of what users expect from a toggle.

Fix: restyle to match the 'Filter By Distance' toggle pattern:
  - Label is always 'Zoom View' (static, describes the mode)
  - btn-primary when zoom view is ON, btn-light when OFF
  - fa-icon toggle-on/toggle-off prefix (same as Filter By Distance)

Before (current):
  <button [ngClass]="{'btn-primary': zoomView, 'btn-outline-secondary': !zoomView}">
    {{zoomView ? 'Standard View' : 'Zoom View'}}
  </button>

After:
  <button [ngClass]="{'btn-primary': zoomView, 'btn-light': !zoomView}">
    <fa-icon *ngIf="zoomView"  icon="toggle-on"  class="mr-2"></fa-icon>
    <fa-icon *ngIf="!zoomView" icon="toggle-off" class="mr-2"></fa-icon>
    Zoom View
  </button>

title: Rename confidence values: color_refined→color, mouse_click→confirmed
description:
## Changes

### 1. color_refined → color  (GCPSighter/refine.py)

Three sites in refine.py:
- Line 339: `"confidence": "color_refined"` → `"color"` (set on successful refinement)
- Line 382: `est.get("confidence") == "color_refined"` → `== "color"` (R4 post-pass)
- Line 421: `est.get("confidence") == "color_refined"` → `== "color"` (R5 post-pass)

No GCPEditorPro changes needed: `color_refined` is only displayed in the
Confidence column; it is never checked by name in Angular code.

### 2. mouse_click → confirmed  (GCPEditorPro)

Four sites in GCPEditorPro:

**gcps-utils.service.ts**
- Line 194: `imgGcp.confirmed = imgGcp.confidence === "mouse_click"`
  → `imgGcp.confidence === "confirmed" || imgGcp.confidence === "mouse_click"`
  (keep mouse_click as backward-compatible alias for old exported files)
- Line 478: update docstring comment

**images-tagger.component.ts**
- Line 487: `desc.image.confidence = "mouse_click"` → `"confirmed"` (write new value)
- Line 187: `res.confidence === "mouse_click"` → add `|| res.confidence === "mouse_click"`
  for backward compat when loading old files (same pattern as gcps-utils)

No emlid2gcp.py changes needed: the pipeline never writes mouse_click.

## Backward compatibility
- Old gcp_list.txt files with `mouse_click` continue to be recognised as
  confirmed (dual-check in gcps-utils.service.ts and images-tagger).
- Old files with `color_refined` display correctly in the Confidence column

title: rmse_calc.py: read ground truth XY/Z from check file, drop emlid_csv arg
description: rmse_calc.py currently takes three arguments: reconstruction.json, chk_confirmed.txt, and emlid.csv. But chk_confirmed.txt already contains the surveyed XY/Z coordinates (written by prepare_odm.py from the Emlid CSV). rmse_calc.py should read ground truth directly from the check file cols 1-3 (already in the output CRS, e.g. EPSG:32613) instead of re-parsing the Emlid CSV and reprojecting. This removes the redundant third argument, eliminates the parse_survey_csv dependency, and makes the tool self-contained with respect to its input files. Note: the lat/lon reprojection path added in the recent bug fix (069bd91) would be replaced by simply reading the projected coordinates already present in the check file.
close_reason: rmse_calc.py now reads ground truth XYZ directly from check file cols 1-3; emlid_csv argument removed. Verified: synthetic test passes, aztec3 results match expected corridor drift pattern.

title: GCPEditorPro: consider hiding distance filter in zoom view
description:
The 'filter by distance' toggle is designed for the grid view, where the user
may have hundreds of images loaded and wants to narrow down to those near the GCP.
In the zoom view the context is different:

- Images are already pre-selected by the pipeline (only images with estimates shown)
- Ordering is by confidence (best-centered / nadir-first), not by distance
- The user is meant to work sequentially through the list — a distance filter
  that silently drops images mid-sequence disrupts that workflow
- Pipeline estimates are only generated for images within the footprint radius,
  so gross-distance filtering is already done upstream

## Options to evaluate

1. **Hide the filter controls entirely in zoom view** — simplest; the toggle and
   slider are not shown when zoomView === true. filterImages() already branches on
   zoomView, so the filter can simply be skipped (apply no distance filter) in
   that branch.

2. **Keep controls visible but disable/grey them out** — less clean, but makes
   it clear the feature exists and is intentionally inactive in this mode.

3. **Keep as-is** — if testing reveals users actually want distance filtering
   in zoom view (e.g. when images are loaded manually rather than from a pipeline
   file), reconsider.

## Recommendation
Option 1. The zoom view has a clear purpose (sequential confirmation of
pipeline estimates); distance filtering is noise. The grid view keeps the
feature unchanged.

## Implementation
- images-tagger.component.ts: filterImages() already has a zoomView branch —
  in that branch, do not apply distance filter (already the case since the
  zoom branch just does filtered = rawImages.slice() without the distance check...
  actually check this — the current zoom branch DOES apply the distance filter
  via the 'filtered' variable computed before the branch).
- images-tagger.component.html: conditionally hide the filter toggle UI when
  zoomView is true.
close_reason: Distance filter hidden in zoom view (template + filterImages logic). Committed GCPEditorPro feature/auto-gcp-pipeline 026a5d7

title: Crosshair style: use crosshair.png in sub-images, black=unconfirmed green=confirmed
description:
Two inconsistencies to fix:

1. SUB-IMAGE CANVAS USES + LINES, LARGE IMAGE USES crosshair.png
Sub-image crop component draws canvas lines (+ shape). Large image uses crosshair.png overlay.
Fix: load crosshair.png at module level (const _crosshairImg = new Image(); _crosshairImg.src = 'assets/crosshair.png').
In _draw(): draw the PNG with ctx.filter for color, centered at GCP pixel position (px-16, py-16) since crosshair is 32x32.
Filter: confirmed = 'hue-rotate(100deg) saturate(3) brightness(1.2)' (green), else 'brightness(0)' (black).

2. COLOR CONVENTION
Current: yellow=unconfirmed estimate, green=confirmed.
New: black=unconfirmed/estimated, green=confirmed (manually placed).
The yellow convention is harder to see and less intuitive than black.

Changes needed:
- smartimage.component.html: filter ternary from '...green...' : 'none' to '...green...' : 'brightness(0)'
- smartimage.component.ts: default pinColor = 'black' (was 'yellow')
- images-tagger.component.html: update [pinColor] bindings to use 'black' for unconfirmed in both standard and zoom views
- sub-image-crop.component.ts: replace canvas line drawing with crosshair.png draw + ctx.filter

Files: smartimage.component.html, smartimage.component.ts, sub-image-crop.component.ts, images-tagger.component.html
notes: crosshair.png is 32x32, lives at assets/crosshair.png. ctx.filter works in Chromium (Electron). Black=unconfirmed is more legible than yellow.
close_reason: Implemented: crosshair.png drawn in sub-image canvas. Final direction: no color filter on crosshair in any view — unmodified icon everywhere. Border colors on sub-image crops continue to indicate confirmed/estimated/untagged state.

title: three-class GCP system: control / check / spare
description:
Extend the existing two-class GCP system (GCP-* control, CHK-* check) to a
full three-class system adding spare/extra points (SPA-* prefix).

## Motivation
Gives users a clear tagging priority:
  - Control (GCP-*): tag first, ≥7 images — fed to ODM bundle adjustment
  - Check   (CHK-*): tag second, ≥3 images — withheld for independent RMSE
  - Spare   (SPA-*): skip unless time permits — not currently used in processing

## Class storage
The GCP name prefix IS the class (already true for GCP- and CHK-). Add SPA-
as the third prefix. No new storage columns required; classes round-trip
through gcp_list.txt naturally.

## Default classification (emlid2gcp.py)
Add optional --classify flag:
  ranks  1-7  → rename to GCP-{label}
  ranks  8-14 → rename to CHK-{label}
  ranks 15+   → rename to SPA-{label}
Off by default to preserve existing naming behavior.
Numbers (7 / 7) are configurable via --n-control N --n-check N.

## GCPEditorPro changes

### Map pins (gcps-map.component.ts)
Replace current isTopGcp logic (gcpIndex < 7) with isControl (name starts
with 'GCP-'). Labeled colored pin = control. Separate distinct icon for
check points (already partly done with cp-icon-2x.png). Spare points use
standard blue pin.

### GCP list (gcps-map.component.html)
Show a class badge next to each GCP name: CTL / CHK / SPA.
Badge color: CTL = blue/primary, CHK = teal/info, SPA = secondary/grey.

### Reclassification UI
Expand the existing 'Checkpoint:' checkbox in the map popup to a 3-way
inline selector: [Control] [Check] [Spare]. Clicking a class renames the
GCP with the appropriate prefix (same rename logic as existing CHK toggle,
extended to cover all three classes). Preserve the prevNames history for
undo-ability.

### Summary line (gcps-map.component.html)
Update the 'N/7 top GCPs sufficiently tagged' line to:
  'N/M control GCPs sufficiently tagged'
where M = number of GCP-* points (not hardcoded 7).

### Export (export-config, see geo-ksv)
gcp_control.txt — confirmed GCP-* rows
gcp_check.txt   — confirmed CHK-* rows
(SPA-* rows excluded from both; included in gcp_list.txt as always)

## Relationship to geo-ksv
geo-ksv (split export) should be implemented as part of or after this bead,
since the export split is predicated on the three-class naming being in place.

## Related
- geo-ksv: split confirmed export into control and check files
- geo-r2f: tagging guide — workflow documentation
- docs/tagging-guide.md, gcp_object_identification.md
close_reason: Python pipeline complete: _classify_gcps(), --classify/--n-control/--n-check flags. GCPEditorPro UI changes tracked in unblocked beads geo-ksv, geo-r2f.

title: Add spot interruption telemetry: 2-min warning annotation + resume tracking
description:
Poll EC2 spot termination metadata endpoint during ODM runs and fire Grafana annotations + log entries on interruption warning and pipeline resume. Gives clean before/after markers in Grafana timeline for cost/rework analysis.

## Changes needed

### odm-monitor.sh (or odm-run.sh)
Add a background poller that checks the spot termination endpoint every 5s:
  http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/spot/termination-time
- On 2-minute warning: fire annotate_grafana() with text '⚡ spot interruption warning' tag=odm,spot
- Log to /var/log/odm-bootstrap.log so Loki captures it
- Write /tmp/odm-spot-interrupted with timestamp (so @reboot resume can detect it)

### odm-run.sh (@reboot / resume path)
- On startup, check for /tmp/odm-spot-interrupted
- If present: fire annotate_grafana() '↩ resumed after spot interruption' tag=odm,spot
- Log interruption count (increment /var/log/odm-interruption-count)
- Remove /tmp/odm-spot-interrupted after logging

### Grafana
- Interruption annotations will appear as vertical markers in dashboards
- Gap between warning and resume = total downtime per interruption

## Notes
- Termination endpoint returns 404 when no interruption pending; HTTP 200 + timestamp = warning issued
- Poller should be a background subshell in odm-monitor.sh, not blocking
- annotate_grafana() already exists in odm-run.sh — reuse it
- On non-spot (on-demand) instances the endpoint always 404s — poller is harmless
close_reason: Implemented: background poller + resume detection in odm-run.sh, committed 893a18a, uploaded to S3

title: odm-run.sh: use cameras.json prior if present in S3 project
description:
If a cameras.json file exists in the S3 project prefix, pass it to ODM via --cameras flag to seed bundle adjustment with a prior calibration from a previous run of the same drone.

**Why**: Brown model is stable across sites for fixed-focal drone cameras. Seeding BA with a good prior improves convergence and reduces risk of local minima — particularly useful for long corridors with sparse GCPs.

**ODM flag**: --cameras /datasets/project/cameras.json
Camera key must match (derived from make/model/resolution/lens type — identical for same drone).

**Implementation in odm-bootstrap.sh or odm-run.sh**:
- After s3 sync of project dir (which pulls cameras.json if present), check if /data/project/cameras.json exists
- If yes, append --cameras /datasets/project/cameras.json to ODM_FLAGS before running stages
- cameras.json is written by ODM after the dataset stage — so it will be present on resume runs automatically; for first runs it needs to be pre-seeded from a prior job

**Workflow for operator**:
- After a successful run: cameras.json is already in S3 at s3://stratus-jrstear/{PROJECT}/cameras.json
- For a new job with the same drone: copy cameras.json to the new project prefix before terraform apply
- odm-run.sh picks it up automatically

**Note**: ODM may update cameras.json during the run (refining the model) — this is fine and desirable.
close_reason: Implemented in odm-bootstrap.sh — appends --cameras flag when cameras.json present in project dir after S3 sync. Committed in geo main.

title: Research: ODM/WebODM check-point workflow and RMSE deliverable format
description:
## Findings (researched 2026-03-05)

### ODM / WebODM side
- **ODM has NO check-point concept** in gcp_list.txt — all rows are treated as control points and used in bundle adjustment.
- There is no separate input file or flag for check points in ODM.
- ODM outputs a report.pdf and georeferencing/GCP residuals (residuals.json/csv) but only for the points supplied as control — no held-out projection is computed.

### GCPEditorPro side
- The CHK-* / GCP-* classification exists ONLY in GCPEditorPro. It does not affect what ODM does.
- The WebODM auto-gcp plugin passes all confirmed tags to ODM; no GCP-*/CHK-* filtering occurs.
- The Checkpoint toggle in GCPEditorPro renames GCP-↔CHK- in storage, but both are exported identically.

### RMSE calculation side
- Isaiah's RMSE deliverable requires manual post-processing: surveyed XYZ vs photogrammetric XYZ.
- Pix4D marks.csv (geo-if2) is the most likely input, since it contains pixel projections with image filenames.
- No automated check-point residual pipeline exists yet; manual comparison is currently acceptable.

### Conclusion
- Implementing a 'true' check-point workflow (held-out from bundle adjustment) would require ODM changes — out of scope.
- The GCP-*/CHK-* naming is a user-facing organizational tool only.

title: EC2 resource telemetry: node_exporter + Grafana Alloy → Grafana Cloud
description:
Collect per-second CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network metrics from EC2 ODM instances and ship to Grafana Cloud for analysis. AWS CloudWatch only exposes CPU; memory is critical (aztec/aztec2 crashes were OOM) and not visible without an agent.

**Stack choice**:
- node_exporter — standard Linux metrics (CPU/core, MemAvailable, disk throughput, net)
- Grafana Alloy (formerly Grafana Agent) — scrapes node_exporter, remote_writes to Grafana Cloud. No separate Prometheus server needed; Alloy is a single binary, works well on ephemeral instances.
- Grafana Cloud free tier: 10k active series, 14-day retention — sufficient for a single instance and a few jobs

**user-data additions** (odm-bootstrap.sh or separate script):
- Install node_exporter (systemd unit, scrape interval 15s)
- Install Grafana Alloy; write alloy config pointing to Grafana Cloud remote_write endpoint
- Alloy config pulls GRAFANA_CLOUD_URL and GRAFANA_CLOUD_API_KEY from /etc/odm-env (injected by Terraform from vars/SSM)
- Add instance label: {job='odm', project=PROJECT, instance_type=INSTANCE_TYPE}

**Terraform additions**:
- variable grafana_cloud_url, grafana_cloud_api_key (sensitive, written to /etc/odm-env)
- These can be left empty to skip telemetry (no-op if Alloy config absent)

**Key metrics to capture**:
- node_memory_MemAvailable_bytes, node_memory_MemTotal_bytes (OOM risk)
- node_cpu_seconds_total per-core (saturation)
- node_disk_read/write_bytes_total (I/O bound stages)
- node_network_receive/transmit_bytes_total (S3 sync cost)
- Docker container CPU (docker stats via cAdvisor, or manual scrape) — per-container breakdown

**Dashboard**: basic panels for memory%, CPU%, disk I/O rate, net rate. Time range = job duration. Job label allows overlaying multiple runs.

**Substage resolution within opensfm**: opensfm logs emit substage markers (detect_features, match_features, reconstruct, undistort). A lightweight log-tailer sidecar (or simple grep in odm-run.sh between polls) can push Grafana annotations at each substage boundary, giving sub-stage resolution without modifying ODM.

**Related**: geo-c5x (cost overlay dashboard), geo-8fg (stage switching — this data directly informs that decision), geo-6sm (RMSE after opensfm)

title: Research: DJI lens calibration to minimize distortion (banana effect)
description:
Research best practices and tooling for maximizing lens calibration accuracy for DJI drones, with the goal of minimizing radial distortion artifacts (e.g. 'banana effect' in flat terrain reconstructions).

## Background
ODM/OpenSfM self-calibrates from the dataset, but self-calibration can be unreliable — especially with nadir-only flight patterns where there is little variation in viewing angle. Poor calibration produces systematic doming/bowling/banana distortion in the point cloud and DSM.

## Questions to Answer
1. **Calibration target methods**: Checkerboard / charuco board imaging — what protocol? How many images, what angles, distance? Indoor vs outdoor?
2. **Software options**: OpenCV (cv2.calibrateCamera), Kalibr, GML Camera Calibration Toolbox, ODM's own --camera-lens flag, Agisoft Metashape calibration, others?
3. **DJI-specific**: Does DJI provide factory calibration files? Are they accurate? Which DJI models (Mavic 3, M300 etc) have known distortion profiles already in ODM?
4. **Integration with ODM**: How to supply a pre-calibrated camera model to ODM? (--cameras JSON, prior-cameras.json). What parameters does ODM accept?
5. **Flight protocol alternatives**: Can cross-grid (nadirs + obliques) or varying altitude flights substitute for physical calibration targets?
6. **Validation**: How to quantify improvement — GCP residuals, DSM flatness over known flat surface?

## Deliverables
- Decision: checkerboard protocol + software recommendation
- Step-by-step calibration procedure for our DJI workflow
- How to inject calibration into ODM pipeline
- Validation method against known flat surface or GCP residuals

title: Lambda purchase-option switching: spot ↔ on-demand via EBS detach/attach
description:
Extend the stage-switch Lambda (geo-8fg) to also change purchase option (spot → on-demand and back). Enables on-demand for reconstruction substage to eliminate spot interruption risk.

**Mechanism:**
1. Instance tags itself with odm:next-instance-type AND odm:next-purchase (spot|on-demand)
2. Instance shuts down
3. Lambda reads tags. If purchase option changes:
   a. Detach EBS root volume from stopped instance
   b. Terminate old instance
   c. Launch new instance with desired purchase option + instance type
   d. Stop new instance, swap root EBS, start
4. Bootstrap resumes from completed stages as normal

**EBS consideration:** Currently the root volume IS the data volume (/data is on root EBS). This simplifies the detach/attach — just swap the entire root volume. No separate data volume needed. ODM, Docker, scripts etc. are all on the same EBS so no reinstall is needed after the swap.

**Complexity:** ~120 lines of Lambda vs ~60 for type-only switching. Main addition is the launch-new-instance flow with proper VPC/SG/IAM/key configuration (must mirror the original instance).

**Alternative considered:** Separate data EBS volume. Rejected because it requires reinstalling ODM/Docker on each new instance (root volume is fresh AMI). Using root EBS as the persistent volume avoids this — everything survives the swap.

**Depends on:** geo-8fg (basic stage switching must work first)

title: Self-hosted Grafana/Prometheus/Loki stack on AWS
description:
Replace Grafana Cloud with self-hosted open-source stack on AWS to get unlimited retention at ~$1/month vs ~$14.50/month on paid Cloud plan.

## Motivation
- Grafana Cloud unlimited trial expires ~2026-04-09 (12 days from 2026-03-28)
- Paid Cloud plan: $6.50/1k metric series + $8/user/month = ~$14.50/month minimum
- Self-hosted: t3.small ~$0.21/hr × ~5 hrs/run × 4 runs/month + EBS ≈ ~$1/month
- Key driver: long-term historical access to all run data (Grafana Cloud free tier is 14-day retention; paid is 13 months but expensive)

## Stack (all open source)
- **Grafana** (Apache 2.0) — dashboards, already have odm-pipeline.json to re-import
- **Prometheus** (Apache 2.0) — metrics storage, remote_write target for ODM EC2
- **Loki** (AGPL) — log aggregation (optional; lower priority than metrics)
- **Alloy / node_exporter** — already deployed on ODM EC2, just change remote_write URL

## Architecture
- Single t3.small EC2 (2 vCPU, 2 GB) running all three services via docker-compose
- Persistent EBS volume (20–50 GB gp3) for Prometheus TSDB + Loki chunks — survives instance stop/start
- Terraform module in infra/monitoring/ — separate from infra/ec2/ (ODM pipeline)
- Instance starts automatically when ODM job launches (terraform output or dependency); can be started independently for dashboard review
- Instance stops after review; EBS persists all historical data
- Static Elastic IP so remote_write URL is stable across stop/start cycles

## Key constraint
ODM EC2 pushes metrics via remote_write during run — monitoring stack must be UP for the entire run or metrics are lost. Terraform apply for a new ODM job should include or trigger the monitoring stack start.

## Prometheus retention
Configure --storage.tsdb.retention.time=2y (or unlimited) — no cloud cost for long retention.

## Migration steps
1. Build infra/monitoring/ Terraform module (EC2, EBS, EIP, security group, docker-compose user-data)
2. Update odm-progress.sh / Grafana Alloy config: swap remote_write URL from Grafana Cloud to self-hosted Prometheus
3. Re-import infra/grafana/odm-pipeline.json into self-hosted Grafana
4. Update memory/reference_grafana.md with new endpoints
5. Tear down Grafana Cloud access policy (or just let trial expire)

## Cost estimate
- t3.small on-demand: $0.0208/hr
- EBS 30 GB gp3: $2.40/month
- Elastic IP (while stopped): $0.005/hr idle — consider allocating only when instance is up, or accept ~$3.60/month to keep EIP reserved
- Typical: 4 runs/month × 8 hrs/run = 32 hrs × $0.0208 = $0.67 + EBS $2.40 ≈ **$3/month**
- vs Grafana Cloud paid: ~$14.50/month

## Deadline
Trial expires ~2026-04-09. Either implement before then or downgrade to Grafana Cloud free tier (14-day retention, 10k series — acceptable as interim).

title: rmse.py: support Pix4D inputs for cross-platform accuracy comparison
description:
Extend rmse.py to accept Pix4D project outputs alongside ODM, enabling side-by-side accuracy comparison in the same report format.

**Pix4D equivalents:**
| ODM input | Pix4D equivalent |
|---|---|
| reconstruction.topocentric.json | Pix4D calibrated camera params (params/ dir) |
| gcp_list.txt / chk_list.txt | Pix4D marks file (tie_points_marks.txt) with GCP/CHK labels |
| odm_orthophoto.tif | Pix4D orthomosaic (exports/*.tif) |

**Key differences to handle:**
- Pix4D marks file format: different column layout, coordinate conventions
- Pix4D camera model: may use different distortion model than OpenSFM brown
- Pix4D reconstruction: no single JSON file — calibrated params are per-camera text files
- CRS: Pix4D may output in project CRS directly (no topocentric frame)

**Possible approaches:**
A. Add --pix4d-project flag that reads Pix4D directory structure directly
B. Write a converter: pix4d_to_odm.py that translates Pix4D outputs to ODM format, then run rmse.py unchanged
C. Abstract the triangulation interface: rmse.py accepts a 'project' that can be ODM or Pix4D

Option B is simplest — a thin adapter layer, rmse.py stays clean.

**Value:** Direct comparison of ODM vs Pix4D accuracy on the same dataset using the same methodology and report format. Relevant for the paper (geo-private-kf8) and for customer confidence when transitioning from Pix4D to ODM.

**Data available:** Red Rocks has both Pix4D and ODM outputs. Aztec has ODM only.

title: true_ortho.py: --compare mode producing both no-DSM and with-DSM outputs in single pass

- true_orthophoto_nodm.tif (best-nadir camera, no occlusion check)
- true_orthophoto_dsm.tif (best-nadir-unoccluded camera)

Both share ~85% of the work:
- Reconstruction loading, coordinate conversion, camera sorting, image reading
- Only the DSM output adds the occlusion ray-march per pixel

title: Benchmark parallelism on large dataset and evaluate scaling options
description:
The pipeline currently has two parallelism contexts:

1. FOOTPRINT MATCHING (match_images_to_gcps):
   - Uses ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=threads)
   - Work is pure Python math (no I/O) — GIL limits true parallelism
   - For N images x M GCPs: O(N*M) distance checks — trivially fast even sequentially
   - WebODM path uses threads=1 (sequential) to avoid gunicorn deadlock

2. PIXEL PROJECTION (_compute_estimates_mode_a / run_pipeline Mode B):
   - Currently sequential loop — no parallelism at all
   - Mode A: pure math, trivially fast, parallelism unlikely to matter
   - Mode B: involves scipy Rotation.from_rotvec per image — more expensive, potentially worth parallelizing

EXPERIMENT GOALS:
1. Benchmark on a large dataset (100+ images, 10+ GCPs):
   - Measure wall time for each stage (EXIF read, footprint match, pixel projection, output write)
   - Identify which stage dominates
   - Measure exiftool batch call time (likely I/O-bound, but exiftool itself is fast)

2. Evaluate footprint match parallelism:
   - Test threads=1 vs threads=N for N in [2,4,8,cpu_count]
   - Is the GIL actually limiting? (footprint math is pure Python, so likely yes)
   - If GIL-limited: consider ProcessPoolExecutor for footprint match in CLI context
     (OK for CLI; still not OK inside gunicorn)

3. Evaluate pixel projection parallelism:
   - Profile Mode B per image: is scipy Rotation expensive enough to parallelize?
   - If yes: add ThreadPoolExecutor to _compute_estimates_mode_b() in CLI context

4. Evaluate exiftool parallelism:
   - Currently one batch call for all images (already optimal — single exiftool invocation)
   - No further optimization needed

5. Recommend whether any parallelism improvements are worth implementing, and at
   what dataset scale they become meaningful.

CONTEXT:
- WebODM always uses threads=1 (sequential) — do not change this
- CLI uses threads=0 (all CPUs) by default — this is where parallelism matters
- GIL note: ThreadPoolExecutor is fork-safe but GIL-limited for pure Python CPU work;
  ProcessPoolExecutor gives true parallelism but risks deadlock if used inside gunicorn

Output: update this bead with findings and create follow-up beads if improvements are warranted.
notes: Stage timing analysis first. Footprint match is O(N*M) trivial math; projection is where Mode B cost lives. CLI vs WebODM context matters.

description:

title: Visibility-aware camera selection for orthophoto generation
description:
True orthophoto via post-processing: replace occlusion-affected pixels using visibility-aware camera selection.

**Problem:** ODM selects the most-nadir camera per pixel without occlusion checking. Near vegetation/structures, the selected camera may see canopy instead of ground, causing positional artifacts and visual errors.

**Approach (validated against Pix4D 'true ortho', Habib 2007, Rau 2002):**

Post-process the existing ODM orthophoto — no re-run needed.

1. Load ODM orthophoto as starting canvas
2. Compute occlusion_mask = (DSM - DTM) > threshold → marks where trees/structures exist
3. Dilate mask slightly (off-nadir viewing affects nearby ground pixels)
4. For ONLY masked pixels (~10-20% of image):
   a. Look up ground_z from DTM at (x, y)
   b. Sort cameras by nadir angle to (x, y, ground_z)
   c. Ray-march from camera to ground point, sampling DSM along the ray
   d. If all DSM samples below ray → unoccluded → project through camera model → sample undistorted image
   e. Replace the ODM ortho pixel with the corrected value
   f. If no camera has clear view → keep ODM pixel (best available)
5. Feather/blend at mask boundaries to avoid seams

**Key advantages:**
- 90% of pixels untouched (open ground is fine)
- No ODM re-run — consumes standard outputs: DTM, DSM, reconstruction.topocentric.json, undistorted images, orthophoto
- Pure Python: GDAL raster I/O + pinhole projection (undistorted images = no lens model needed) + cv2 sampling
- Measurable A/B via rmse_visual_report.py on both orthos
- Average ~1.2 camera checks/pixel (early termination), not N_cameras

**Inputs (all standard ODM outputs):**
- odm_dem/dtm.tif, odm_dem/dsm.tif
- opensfm/reconstruction.topocentric.json (camera positions + models)
- opensfm/undistorted/images/*.jpg
- odm_orthophoto/odm_orthophoto.original.tif (canvas to patch)

**Output:** true_orthophoto.tif — same extent/resolution, occlusion-corrected

**Script:** accuracy_study/true_ortho.py or similar
close_reason: Proof of concept validated: true ortho shows significant improvement at survey targets. Tiled full-image mode + multiprocessing added (geo-ksd).

title: emlid2gcp.py: pixel refinement by locating X-marker center in image
description:
After the pipeline projects a GCP to an estimated pixel location, refine that
estimate to the precise center of the NEAREST X marker, and compute the tight
bounding box of the full marker extent.  Results written back to gcp_list.txt
as updated px/py and a new marker_bbox column consumed by GCPEditorPro (geo-s6p)
for adaptive zoom cropping and by training-data extractors.

See docs/gcp_object_identification.md (Stage 3) for full detail.

## Pipeline position
Runs after GCP and image ordering (highest-priority images refined first).
Each (GCP, image) pair is independent → ThreadPoolExecutor.
Controlled by --refine-pixels flag (off by default until validated).

## Algorithm (four steps per image)

### Step 1 — Anomaly-relative color detection
Flag pixels that deviate from local background colour distribution (sampled
from surrounding ring/annulus). Fallback: HSV masks for orange/red/white
tried in sequence.

### Step 2 — Search: expanding annuli (nearest-first)
Seed from (px, py). Expand outward until candidate marker pixels found.
Hard cap ~300 px; fall back to unrefined estimate if exceeded.
Nearest-first guarantees correctness in multi-GCP images.

### Step 3 — Capture: flood-fill to full marker extent
Flood-fill connected non-natural pixels from first hit (no hard boundary).
Compute tight axis-aligned bounding box of connected component.
Sanity-check: discard if too small (noise), too large (merged markers),
or aspect ratio inconsistent with an X shape.

### Step 4 — Localisation: arm-cluster line intersection
The centroid of the colored pixel set is NOT the correct intersection when
arms differ in length or are partially occluded:
1. PCA on colored pixels → two principal-axis directions.
2. Cluster pixels by dominant eigenvector → arm-1 pixels, arm-2 pixels.
3. Fit independent line through each cluster (cv2.fitLine).
4. Geometric intersection of the two fitted lines → refined (px, py).
Fallback: skeleton branching point (skimage.morphology.skeletonize).

## Output — updated gcp_list.txt columns

When refinement succeeds the row is updated:
  col 4 (px)          → refined sub-pixel X
  col 5 (py)          → refined sub-pixel Y
  col 8 (confidence)  → 'color_refined'
  col 9 (marker_bbox) → "x1,y1,x2,y2" comma-separated integer pixel coords
                         of tight bounding box around full marker extent

Col 9 absent for unrefined rows (backward compatible).
Bbox is tight connected-component fit with no padding; consumers add margin.

## Dependencies
- opencv-python (cv2)
- scikit-image (skimage.morphology.skeletonize) — skeleton fallback

## Related
- docs/gcp_object_identification.md — full Stage 3 spec
- geo-s6p — GCPEditorPro adaptive crop using marker_bbox column
- geo-bwb — training data (marker_bbox provides positive-sample crops)
- geo-d83 / geo-kit — CNN classifiers (Stage 4, deferred; uses this output)
- geo-70t — checkerboard/circle refinement (depends on this bead)
close_reason: Implemented in emlid2gcp.py across commits ecaccbb, 502fe64, 8bd1173. Scoring: ratio×(λ_min+λ_max)×mean_dev×coherence. Further tuning tracked separately.

title: transform.py dc: write anchor_x/y as null instead of control centroid
description:
transform.py dc currently computes the centroid of control monuments and writes
it as anchor_x/anchor_y in transform.yaml. This is not needed: anchors only
matter when scale != 1.0 (pure Helmert), and in practice anchors are 0,0.

## Change
In cmd_dc(), remove the centroid computation (lines ~376-379) and write
anchor_x: null, anchor_y: null in design_grid instead.

## Desired downstream effect

  (already filtered by `not in ("", "null")` check) → fields stay empty in GUI
- app.py /run: add_arg("anchor_x"/"anchor_y") will find no value → not included
  in CLI invocation (already suppressed at scale=1 anyway, but null ensures
  they never appear even if scale is manually overridden)
- package.py: args.anchor_x/anchor_y stay at default 0.0 → correct behavior

## Verify

2. Confirm transform.yaml has anchor_x: null, anchor_y: null
3. Load in GUI → anchor fields empty
4. Run → --anchor-x/--anchor-y absent from invocation

title: Draw sub-image crop bounding box on large image in zoom view
description:
In the zoom view's large image panel, draw a colored bounding box showing the
region that the currently-selected sub-image crop covers.

## What to draw
The sub-image crop is a 256×256 px window centered on the GCP pixel (imX, imY),
clamped to image bounds (same logic as SubImageCropComponent._draw):
  sx = max(0, min(imX - 128, imgW - 256))
  sy = max(0, min(imY - 128, imgH - 256))
  rect = (sx, sy, 256, 256)

## Color
Matches the sub-image border color (same as SubImageCropComponent.borderColor):
  confirmed → green  (#28a745)
  estimated → yellow (#ffc107)
If isTagged is false (manually added image, not yet clicked), skip the box.

## Implementation approach
1. Add @Input() cropBox: {x,y,w,h,color} | null to SmartimageComponent.
2. Inside the panzoom container div (parent of the img), add a positioned overlay
   div using left/top/width/height in image-pixel space — it shares the panzoom
   transform so it scales/pans correctly with the image.
3. In images-tagger.component.html, bind cropBox on the zoom-view app-smartimage:
   [cropBox]="selectedDesc ? getCropBox(selectedDesc) : null"
4. Add getCropBox(desc) in images-tagger.component.ts: reads imX/imY, gets image
   natural dimensions from the SmartimageComponent @ViewChild, computes sx/sy.

## Behaviour
- Box updates whenever selectedDesc changes (hover → new sub-image selected).
- Click to reposition GCP still works unchanged; box moves to new position.
close_reason: Implemented and committed (34e296d): syncCropBox() in SmartimageComponent draws 256×256 bounding box on large image matching sub-image crop color (green=confirmed, yellow=estimated). Tracks pan/zoom.

title: GCP placement: flight coverage filter
description:
Given a recommended flight altitude (derived from RMSE target — see geo-9dk and geo-8sk), overlap, and camera FOV, compute theoretical image count at each GCP candidate location.

KEY TRADE-OFF TO SURFACE: The altitude required to hit the RMSE target may conflict
with flight time or image count budgets. When flagging a candidate as under-covered
(< 7 images), the tool should indicate whether the fix is:
  (a) setback the end anchor (preferred — free, no extra images)
  (b) increase overlap at the corridor ends (more images, longer flight)
  (c) accept lower altitude everywhere (more images, longer flight, better RMSE)

SPECIFIC OUTPUTS:
  - Per-candidate estimated image count at recommended altitude + overlap
  - Flag candidates with < 7 estimated images (primarily end-of-corridor anchors)
  - Recommended end-anchor setback distance to clear the 7-image threshold
  - If setback is not feasible (corridor too short), suggest overlap increase

Display in WebODM UI. Does not auto-move candidates — user decides.
Depends on: WebODM UI bead (geo-9dk).

title: odm-run.sh: detect and clean corrupted opensfm state on spot resume
description:
When spot interrupts opensfm during reconstruction, it leaves corrupted intermediate state (partial reconstruction.json, incomplete tracks). On resume, opensfm tries to build on this corrupted state and crashes quickly.

**Immediate fix:** In odm-run.sh, before re-running opensfm after a spot resume, check for signs of corrupted state:
- reconstruction.json exists but is truncated / invalid JSON
- tracks.csv exists but opensfm still reports as incomplete
- profile.log shows reconstruction was in progress when interrupted

If corruption detected: delete opensfm reconstruction outputs (keep features/ and matches/ which are complete and reusable), then re-run. This avoids the cascading failure.

**Root cause:** opensfm's incremental reconstruction writes state incrementally without atomic commits. A kill mid-write produces invalid state that the next run can't recover from.

**Evidence:** aztec7 2026-03-31: Run 2 killed by spot at 06:50 during alignment (1168 cameras). Run 3 crashed 90s into reconstruction. Manual cleanup + restart with clean features/matches succeeded.
close_reason: Implemented: reconstruction.json validity check before opensfm resume. Invalid JSON → clean reconstruction state, keep features/matches.

title: Verify GCPEditorPro confirmed-GCP download matches pipeline; unify format
description:
## Goal
Confirm that the file downloaded from GCPEditorPro after a user confirms GCPs
matches what gcp_pipeline.py produces (same coordinates, same images), and make
the two formats identical so a plain `diff` can be used for comparison.

## Current format mismatch (observed 2026-02-26)

### gcpeditpro.txt (pipeline, _write_gcp_list):
  - Space-separated columns
  - 4 decimal places with trailing zeros: 1666970.6970  1596792.0180  6355.1720
  - Pixel coords also padded: 1813.30  536.20

### gcp_list.txt (GCPEditorPro download):
  - Tab-separated columns
  - Trailing zeros stripped: 1666970.697  1596792.018  6355.172  1813.3  536.2

## Fix (gcp_pipeline.py _write_gcp_list)
1. Change separator from spaces to tabs (match GCPEditorPro)
2. Strip trailing zeros from geo coords and pixel coords, e.g.:
     f"{v:.3f}".rstrip('0').rstrip('.')   # geo coords (mm precision)
     f"{v:.2f}".rstrip('0').rstrip('.')   # pixel coords

## Verification workflow
After fixing the format:
  1. Run pipeline on a real dataset → gcpeditpro.txt
  2. Load gcpeditpro.txt into GCPEditorPro, confirm a few GCPs, download gcp_list.txt
  3. Sort both files (excluding header) and diff — should show only the confirmed/corrected rows
     (the actual px/py will differ for confirmed GCPs; unconfirmed rows should be identical)
close_reason: Format unified (tabs, trailing-zero stripping) in commit cc36175. Verified with diff after GCPEditorPro confirm: exactly one line differs (the clicked image), all others identical. Bonus: discovered and fixed two bugs in images-tagger (load all images OOM, save from filtered list causes data loss) in GCPEditorPro commit 6bd8615.

title: rmse.py: optional uncertainty integration in HTML report
description:
When an uncertainty overlay image is provided (--uncertainty uncertainty.tif), integrate per-point uncertainty values into the HTML report.

**What the uncertainty measures:**
σ_horizontal = σ_DTM × tan(θ_off_nadir) + σ_reconstruction

This is orthophoto positioning uncertainty — how much a feature in the ortho might be laterally shifted from its true ground position. Driven by:
- Off-nadir viewing angle of the camera selected for that pixel (dominant factor)
- DTM accuracy at that location (amplified by viewing angle)
- Base reconstruction accuracy (constant floor, ~0.035m)

NOT directly about number of overlapping images, though correlated — more overlap means ODM can pick a more-nadir camera, reducing uncertainty.

Distinct from rmse.py's dH (triangulation accuracy from ray intersection geometry) and from geo-aw0's per-image spread (tagging consistency).

**Report additions when --uncertainty provided:**
- Summary: uncertainty range (min/max/mean) with brief explanation
- Per-point table: add uncertainty column (sample uncertainty raster at each target's survey coordinate)
- Note the max-uncertainty point and value
- Correlation insight: do high-dH points also have high uncertainty? If yes, the error is geometry-driven. If no, it's tagging-driven.
- Always show the section with explicit values, even when small (explicit knowledge that it was checked)

**CLI:**
  python rmse.py ... --html report.html --uncertainty uncertainty.tif

**Dependency:** Requires uncertainty overlay to be pre-generated (~15 min on laptop, ~16 min on EC2 via ortho_uncertainty.py).

title: odm-run.sh: run rmse_calc after opensfm stage and include results in SNS notification
description:
After the opensfm stage completes, automatically run rmse_calc.py against the check file and include the RMSE summary in the SNS completion notification. This lets the operator assess reconstruction accuracy before committing to the expensive MVS/meshing/ortho stages (~15+ hrs).

**Prerequisite inputs** (must be present in S3 project prefix before run):
- chk_list.txt — CHK-only check file in EPSG:32613 (written by transform.py split)
- gcp_list.txt — GCP control file (already required for ODM)

**Implementation in odm-run.sh**:
- After opensfm stage syncs to S3, check if /data/project/chk_list.txt exists
- If yes: conda run -n geo python /opt/TargetSighter/rmse_calc.py /data/project/opensfm/reconstruction.json /data/project/chk_list.txt
- Capture stdout; parse RMS_X, RMS_Y, RMS_Z (std_dZ), RMS_3D from output
- Include in opensfm SNS notification body (append RMSE table after stage-complete line)
- If chk_list.txt absent: notify 'no check file found — skipping RMSE' (not an error)

**SNS message format** (example):
  Stage opensfm complete on bsn/aztec4.
  
  RMSE vs 11 check points:
    RMS_X  0.08 m   RMS_Y  0.07 m
    std_dZ 0.14 m   RMS_3D 0.17 m
  
  (See S3: bsn/aztec4/opensfm/rmse_report.txt for full per-point table)

- Also write full per-point table to /data/project/opensfm/rmse_report.txt and sync to S3

**rmse_calc.py availability on EC2**:
- TargetSighter/ is already sync'd from S3 or baked into the AMI/user-data; confirm path
- Requires pyproj, numpy — confirm geo conda env or pip install in user-data

**Accuracy targets** (reference, not enforced here — see pause-on-RMSE bead geo-XXX):
- Survey-quality corridor: RMS_X/Y < 0.05 m, std_dZ < 0.07 m (good GCP placement, RTK drone)
- Current aztec3 baseline: RMS_X=0.27m, RMS_Y=0.22m, std_dZ=0.71m (marginal GCP distribution)

**Related**: geo-8fg (stage switching), geo-zjq (cameras.json prior), pause-on-RMSE bead

title: emlid2gcp: simplify to GCP-/CHK-/DUP- (drop SPA-)
description:
## Change
Simplify the three-class system (GCP-/CHK-/SPA-) to two meaningful classes plus
a duplicate marker:

  GCP-*  : first n_control (default 10) by structural sort → control point candidates
  CHK-*  : all remaining non-duplicate marks → check points for RMSE validation
  DUP-*  : near-duplicate marks → listed last

Remove SPA-* entirely. All non-DUP-* marks are worth tagging and refining.

## Naming rationale
Top 10 structural marks are all GCP-* so the name clearly signals their role.
The user tags whichever 7+ are visible; if a top-7 mark is hard to see they
simply tag one of GCP-8..10 instead — no toggling needed. The badge turns green
at 7+ confirmed GCP-*, amber at 3-6, red at 0-2 (unchanged thresholds).

## Output ordering
  Positions  1–10  : GCP-* marks (structural max-insertion sort, all 10)
  Positions  11+   : CHK-* marks, interior-first (see geo-7gx)
  End              : DUP-* marks

## Refinement change
Remove the 'skip SPA-*' optimisation in refine.py. All non-DUP-* marks get
refined. --refine-limit still caps total (GCP, image) pairs processed.

## CLI flag change
--n-control N  (default changed from 7 → 10)

## Badge / coloring (GCPEditorPro)
GCP-* badge: green ≥7, amber 3-6, red 0-2  (count confirmed GCP-* by name prefix,
             not by file position)
CHK-* badge: green ≥3, amber 1-2, red 0    (new, count confirmed CHK-* by prefix)

## Downstream effect
- User toggles GCP-↔CHK- via existing Checkpoint checkbox if desired.
- DUP-* marks appear as unchecked non-CHK in GCPEditorPro.
- geo-ksv: GCP-* rows → gcp_control.txt for ODM; confirmed CHK-* → gcp_check.txt

## Bead impacts
- geo-7fg: soft warning only ('N GCP-* marks are projection-only, consider tagging
  GCP-8..10 instead').
- geo-7gx: interior-first ordering for CHK-* positions 11+.
notes: CHK-* badge thresholds match GCP-*: 0-2=red, 3-6=amber, 7+=green. Simple and consistent.
close_reason: Implemented and committed (5b42726). GCP-/CHK-/DUP- scheme live; DUP-* bug fixed; output messages cleaned up; tagging guide updated with USGS minimums.

title: sight.py: pixel refinement for checkerboard and circle GCP targets
description:
Companion to geo-56c (X-marker refinement). Add localization support for
other common GCP target types:

## Target types

### Checkerboard (e.g. 2×2 or 4×4 black-and-white checker flag)
The precise GCP center is the intersection of the grid corners — specifically
the unique inner corner(s) of the pattern.

Detection and localization approaches:
- **cv2.findChessboardCorners / cv2.findChessboardCornersSB** — standard
  OpenCV; works well on clean prints. Requires knowing the grid dimensions.
- **cv2.cornerSubPix** — sub-pixel refinement of detected corners; directly
  gives the intersection to ~0.1 px accuracy.
- For a 2×2 board (4 squares), there is exactly 1 inner corner — that is the
  GCP center. For larger boards, the center inner corner is the target.
- Robustness: the checker pattern is scale/rotation invariant and works at
  oblique angles. Works in grayscale — no color dependency.

### Circle / bullseye target
The GCP center is the centroid of the innermost circle (or the center of the
bullseye).

Detection and localization approaches:
- **cv2.HoughCircles** — detects circles by radius; center is the GCP location.
- **Contour centroid** — threshold the inner circle color, find contour,
  compute moments for centroid. More robust than Hough on small radii.
- For bullseye: fit concentric circles; use the innermost as the reference.
- Color: inner circle is typically a contrasting color (orange, red, white, or
  black) against a white or black outer ring.

## Auto-detection of target type
Rather than requiring the user to specify the target type, attempt each detector
in sequence within the search region around the rough estimate and accept the
first that returns a high-confidence result:
1. X-marker color detector (geo-56c)
2. Checkerboard corner detector
3. Circle/centroid detector

The order reflects prevalence in the field (X most common for DIY targets,
checkerboard for printed targets, circle for commercial targets).

## Pipeline integration
Same flag as geo-56c: --refine-pixels enables all refinement detectors.
Output confidence label: 'color_refined' (X), 'checker_refined', 'circle_refined'.

## Related
- geo-56c — X-marker refinement (implement first; this bead builds on same
  infrastructure)
- docs/gcp_object_identification.md — update to cover checkerboard and circle
  targets alongside the existing clay-pigeon / spray-paint coverage

title: emlid2gcp: confidence-gated classification (refine-first naming)
description:
## Problem
Currently: sort → classify (rename GCP-*/CHK-*/SPA-*) → refine pixels.
Classification is purely positional — the top 14 by structural sort get named
regardless of whether color refinement can actually find the marker. A geometrically
well-placed but visually faint mark (e.g. GCP-26 in ghostrider) earns GCP- status
even though refinement cannot confirm it. The result is a control point the operator
cannot reliably tag.

## Proposed pipeline order
sort → refine in priority order (all marks, or a generous limit) → classify

Classification gate: a mark is eligible for GCP-*/CHK-* only if its pixel
estimate was updated by refinement (i.e. confidence != 'projection' after R5
bbox-consistency post-pass, meaning the color refinement found and accepted a
marker).  Marks that remain projection-only are SPA- regardless of geometric rank.

## Behavior
- Walk sorted_labels in order.
- First n_control refinement-confident marks → GCP-*
- Next n_check refinement-confident marks → CHK-*
- All others (projection-only OR beyond n_control+n_check confident) → SPA-*
- If fewer than n_control+n_check marks pass refinement, emit a warning and
  assign as many GCP-*/CHK-* as are available.

## Key interaction
- refine.py _postpass_bbox_consistency() already sets confidence back to
  'projection' for rejected detections — use that flag as the confidence gate.
- --refine-limit should default to 'all' (or a large number) when classify=True
  so that SPA-rank marks still get a refinement attempt before classification.

## Files
- GCPSighter/emlid2gcp.py: reorder classify after refine in run_pipeline(); update
  _classify_gcps() to accept a set of 'confident' labels.
- GCPSighter/refine.py: ensure refine_all_estimates() processes all marks (not
  just top-N) when caller needs confidence data for classification.
close_reason: Superseded by geo-6z0 classification redesign. Confidence-gated naming is dropped in favor of simpler GCP-/CHK-/DUP- scheme with no naming gate based on refinement success. Refinement still runs and updates px/py, but all non-top-N non-dup marks become CHK-* regardless of refinement outcome.

title: csv2gcp: interior-first ordering for CHK- positions 11+
description:
## Context (updated from original description)
After the classification simplification (geo-6z0), marks are ordered as:
  1–7    GCP-* (structural perimeter, max-insertion)
  8–10   CHK-* (next-best structural, easy GCP-* substitutes)
  11+    CHK-* (this bead: interior-first ordering)
  end    DUP-*

## Goal
Order the remaining CHK-* marks (positions 11+) so that the marks most useful
for independent RMSE validation appear first in the tagging queue. These should
be interior to the GCP convex hull and well-spaced from each other and from GCPs.

## Why not centroid-proximity
Flight area shapes vary: long corridors, L-shapes, main area + arm (e.g. building
site with attached watershed). The centroid of such shapes may lie outside the
survey area or in a low-value region. 'Interior' must be defined relative to the
actual hull, not a geometric centroid.

## Proposed ordering criterion
Among the CHK-* candidate marks (all non-DUP-*, non-top-10):
1. Score each by: minimum distance to any GCP-* or top-10 CHK-* mark (want
   spacing from already-placed structural marks).
2. Use greedy farthest-point selection: iteratively pick the candidate that
   maximises min-distance to all already-selected marks (and to all GCP-*).
   This naturally spreads check points across the interior of the hull without
   assuming shape.
3. Also require: confident refinement (confidence != 'projection') preferred
   over projection-only — projection-only CHK-* candidates sorted to the end
   of the 11+ block since they are harder to tag accurately.

## Files
- GCPSighter/emlid2gcp.py: new _order_chk_interior() helper called from
  _classify_gcps() after GCP-* and reserve CHK-* (positions 1–10) are fixed.
- scipy.spatial KDTree already available for distance queries.

## Depends on
geo-6z0 (classification simplification — defines the 11+ CHK-* candidate pool)

title: GCPEditorPro: distinguish top-7 GCPs on the map with ratio-colored labelled pins
description:
On the GCP list / map view, visually distinguish the top 7 GCPs (first 7 in
file order = structural priority order from emlid2gcp.py) from the rest.

## Pin design

### Top 7 GCPs
- Distinctive marker type (e.g. numbered circle or star divIcon — different from
  the standard map pin used for ordinary GCPs)
- GCP label displayed next to / on the pin
- Pin color reflects confirmed/total ratio using the same thresholds as geo-lml:
    - Red:   0–2 confirmed
    - Amber: 3–6 confirmed
    - Green: ≥7 confirmed
- Pin updates color live as the user confirms tags and returns to the map

### Other GCPs (8+)
- Standard existing marker (no label, no ratio coloring)
- Behavior unchanged from current

## Intent
The user's spatial goal becomes clear: get all 7 special pins to turn green on
the map. Because the pins are at real survey positions, the user can see the
structural logic intuitively — two distal anchors at opposite corners, Z extremes
on high/low ground, a centre pin, and two perimeter points. No explanation needed.
When all 7 are green the summary line (geo-lml) also turns green — the two
features reinforce each other.

## Implementation notes
- 'Top 7' = first 7 entries in storage.gcps (file-load order, preserved from
  gcp_list.txt)
- Confirmed count per GCP: imageGcps.filter(ig => ig.gcpName === name && ig.confirmed).length
- Shared constant TOP_GCP_COUNT = 7 (same as geo-lml)
- Map is rendered in gcps-map component (Leaflet)
- Use Leaflet divIcon for the top-7 markers (allows HTML/CSS for color + label)
- Color and count must recompute when navigating back from the tagger
  (storage.imageGcps may have changed)

## Dependency
Depends on geo-lml (confirmed/total progress indicators) for shared thresholds
and confirmed-count logic.
notes: Same condition as geo-lml: labelled/colored top-7 pins only shown when hasPipelineEstimates=true. Standard pins for manually-tagged files. GCP list pin style is stateless w.r.t. zoom/grid toggle.

title: RMSE visual report: annotated orthophoto crops per target
description:
Generate an HTML report with annotated orthophoto crops for each GCP/CHK target, sorted by residual magnitude (worst first). Enables rapid visual verification that targets align within their computed RMSE on the orthophoto.

**Inputs:**
- reconstruction.topocentric.json + gcp_list.txt + chk_list.txt → rmse.py JSON output (dX, dY, dH per point)
- {job}_targets.csv → survey coordinates (label, X, Y, Z in EPSG:32613)
- Orthophoto COG (EPSG:32613) → pixel crops around each target

**Per-target crop annotation:**
- Green crosshair: survey coordinate (ground truth)
- Red dot: reconstructed position (survey + dX, dY offset from rmse.py)
- dH circle: radius = horizontal residual, scaled to ortho pixels
- Label + dH/dZ/d3D text overlay

**Output:** Single HTML file with all targets as annotated crop images, sorted worst-first by dH. Job-agnostic (CLI args, no hardcoded paths).

**CLI (proposed):**
    conda run -n geo python accuracy_study/rmse_visual_report.py \
        reconstruction.topocentric.json gcp_list.txt chk_list.txt \
        targets.csv orthophoto.tif -o report.html

**Dependencies:** rmse.py (for JSON output), GDAL (rasterio for COG reads), Pillow or matplotlib for annotation.
close_reason: Visual report implemented with scale circles and upscaled crops. v4 uses corrected rmse.py.

title: verify: similarity fit is near-identity for reconstruction.topocentric.json
description:
Empirically confirm that rmse_calc.py's Umeyama similarity fit produces scale≈1, R≈identity, t≈(0,0,0) when using reconstruction.topocentric.json (post-GCP BA).

## Motivation
The docstring says the similarity fit captures '~25m GPS translation offset and ~1.75° UTM grid convergence.' These numbers likely describe the GPS-era problem (reconstruction.json, pre-GCP BA), not the current state. If reconstruction.topocentric.json is already GCP-georeferenced, the fit should be near-identity.

## If confirmed
- The similarity fit code in rmse_calc.py is vestigial for the topocentric case
- rmse_calc.py can accept chk_list.txt alone (no GCPs needed to georeference)
- Update geo-otx: single-file interface is viable; two-file interface is not required
- Update rmse_calc.py docstring to reflect this accurately
- Consider removing or documenting the similarity fit as a diagnostic-only step

## If not confirmed (fit is significant)
- Understand why: is reference_lla GPS-derived and offset from GCP centroid?
- Two-file interface (geo-otx) stands as designed
- Document the actual magnitude of the residual correction

## How to verify
Run rmse_calc.py on Aztec or Red Rocks with the existing gcp_list.txt.
Add a stderr print of the fitted similarity parameters (scale, rotation angles, |t|).
The code already prints these (lines 528-532) — just run it and read the output.

## Dependency
Needs a completed ODM run with reconstruction.topocentric.json available locally.
notes: RESULT: Similarity fit is NOT near-identity. Running rmse_calc.py with chk_list.txt only (no GCPs → no similarity fit) gave RMS_H = 57.88m / 190 ft. The horizontal errors show a radial pattern (CHK-117 near center = 7 ft, points further out = 215-310 ft), consistent with a rotation + translation systematic offset. Z errors are small (0.37m / 1.2 ft) throughout, showing the vertical axis is not affected. CAVEAT: In this aztec6 run all points (GCP- and CHK-) were used as ODM ground control (geo-uej was never completed), so CHK points are not truly independent here. But the similarity fit magnitude is clear regardless. CONCLUSION: Two-file interface for geo-otx is confirmed correct. The docstring '~25m translation, ~1.75° rotation' is accurate for reconstruction.topocentric.json, not just a GPS-era artifact.
close_reason: Verified: similarity fit IS significant (~58m horizontal error without it). Two-file interface for rmse_calc.py confirmed necessary.

title: Multi-instance stage switching for production ODM runs
description:
Evaluate and implement stage-based instance switching to reduce cost and wall-time on large ODM jobs.

## Background

ODM stages have very different resource profiles:
- Feature extract/match + sparse SfM: multi-core CPU, 8-16 GB RAM, no GPU
- Dense MVS (depth maps): GPU provides 5-10× speedup, 16-32 GB RAM
- Meshing + texturing: RAM-heavy CPU only, 64-128 GB needed
- DEM + orthophoto: modest CPU/IO

A single instance type is always a compromise. Stage switching exploits the fact that
ODM resumes cleanly from any stage via --rerun-from, and EBS volumes survive instance
stop/modify/start unchanged (no snapshot or S3 handoff needed).

## Economic case (Red Rocks, 6.4k images)

| Approach | Cost | Wall time |
|---|---|---|
| r5.8xlarge (no GPU) all stages | ~-101 | 35-50 hr |
| g4dn.12xlarge (GPU) all stages | ~-110 | 18-28 hr |
| Stage-switched (c5 → g4dn.12xlarge → r5.8xlarge) | ~-74 | 14-22 hr |

At Aztec scale (1.4k images) savings are modest (~30-40%, ~-3/run). At Red Rocks
scale (6.4k images) savings are -40/run and meaningful.

## Implementation options

### Option 1: Manual EC2 switching (no code, current approach)
- Stop instance
- aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute --instance-type <next>
- Start instance, resume ODM with --rerun-from <stage>
- Stage boundaries: after opensfm (end sparse) → GPU; after filter points → high-RAM; after texturing → small

### Option 2: Automated orchestration (production scale)
- Step Functions or ECS task chaining
- Trigger instance type change between stage boundaries
- Detect stage completion via ODM log or stage directory presence
- Worth building at 10+ runs/month

## Stage boundaries for switching
1. Stop after opensfm: switch to GPU instance (g4dn.xlarge or g4dn.4xlarge)
2. Stop after odm_filterpoints: switch to RAM-heavy instance (r5.4xlarge or r5.8xlarge)
3. Optional: after texturing, downsize to small for DEM/ortho

## Dependencies / notes
- G quota (on-demand + Spot 64 vCPU) requests submitted; pending approval
- For RMSE experiments: sparse-only (no GPU needed, no stage switching needed)
- See docs/cloud-infra-spec.md Instance Sizing section for full analysis
- Automate only when production volume justifies engineering effort (rule of thumb: 10+ runs/month)
design:
## Revised Plan: Substage-First for OpenSFM

### Motivation
Reconstruction is 60% of cost and 100% of risk. Stage-level switching puts
all of opensfm on one instance type, which either wastes money (on-demand
for features/matching) or risks corruption (spot for reconstruction).

### Implementation: Instance Switching via EC2 ModifyInstanceAttribute

No Step Functions needed for v1. The switching logic lives in odm-run.sh:

```
Phase 1: m5.4xlarge SPOT
  → dataset, opensfm features, matching, tracks
  → Detect: tracks.csv exists → STOP instance

Phase 2: m5.4xlarge ON-DEMAND  
  → ModifyInstanceAttribute (spot → on-demand not possible on same instance)
  → ALTERNATIVE: use a second on-demand instance, or use spot but with
    corruption detection (geo-5sx) as safety net
  → opensfm reconstruct + undistort
  → Detect: undistorted/ complete → STOP instance

Phase 3: g4dn.4xlarge SPOT
  → ModifyInstanceAttribute → start
  → openmvs (GPU accelerated, 5-7x faster)
  → Detect: openmvs complete → STOP instance

Phase 4: m5.4xlarge SPOT  
  → ModifyInstanceAttribute → start
  → odm_filterpoints through odm_report + true_ortho
  → Pipeline complete → cancel spot → shutdown
```

### Critical constraint
EC2 cannot switch between spot and on-demand on the same instance.
ModifyInstanceAttribute only changes instance TYPE, not purchase option.

Options for Phase 2 (on-demand reconstruction):
A. Launch a SEPARATE on-demand instance, attach the EBS volume
B. Stay on spot but implement geo-5sx corruption detection as safety net
C. Use spot with higher max-price to reduce interruption probability

Option B is pragmatic for v1 — spot interruption during reconstruct is
recoverable if we detect and clean corrupted state (geo-5sx).
Option A is the proper fix but requires EBS detach/attach orchestration.

### Cost comparison (aztec scale)

| Approach | Cost | Spot-kill risk |
|---|---|---|
| Current (r5.4xlarge spot all) | ~$4.80 | HIGH — reconstruct lost |
| v1: spot + corruption detect | ~$3.27 | LOW — auto-recovery |  
| v2: spot + on-demand hybrid | ~$3.27 | NONE for reconstruct |

### Implementation order
1. geo-5sx: corruption detection (immediate safety net)
2. Stage switching: m5 spot → g4dn spot → m5 spot (GPU benefit)
3. Later: on-demand for reconstruct via separate instance or Step Functions

### Branch
feature/stage-switching
notes:
URGENT: Spot interruption during opensfm reconstruction caused cascading failure on aztec7 (2026-03-31). Reconstruction is non-resumable — spot kill during alignment corrupts intermediate state, poisoning subsequent runs. Required full cleanup + manual restart.

Timeline: Run 2 got to 1168/1385 cameras, spot killed during alignment at 06:50. Run 3 crashed 90s into reconstruction from corrupted state. ~4 hours wasted + manual intervention required.

**Decision: opensfm reconstruction substage MUST run on-demand.** Feature extraction and matching can tolerate spot (individually fast, restartable). The reconstruction substage is 2+ hours, non-checkpointed, and corruption on interruption requires full restart.

**Proposed stage → instance mapping:**
- dataset: spot (fast, restartable)
- opensfm features/matching: spot (parallelized, restartable)  
- opensfm reconstruct+undistort: ON-DEMAND (2+ hrs, non-resumable, corruption-prone)
- openmvs: spot (can resume)
- odm_filterpoints → odm_report: spot (each <1hr, resumable)
- true_ortho: spot (tiled, restartable)

title: fix: rmse.py should use ODM's geodetic conversion, not fitted similarity
description:
rmse.py fits its own 7-parameter similarity transform to map topocentric → survey CRS. ODM uses a different transform (similarity_transform.json + reference_lla geodetic conversion). These disagree (scale 1.0001567 vs 1.0002537, Z rotation -1.754° vs ~0.57°), so rmse.py's dH values don't correspond to orthophoto positioning.

**Root cause:** A similarity transform is a local approximation of the geodetic conversion (topocentric ENU → ECEF → lat/lon → UTM). With points spread over several km, the approximation breaks down due to UTM grid convergence and scale factor variation.

**Fix:** Replace rmse.py's fitted similarity transform with the proper geodetic conversion chain:
1. Triangulate each target from camera rays in topocentric frame (this part is correct)
2. Convert topocentric ENU → ECEF using reference_lla
3. ECEF → geodetic lat/lon (iterative)
4. lat/lon → UTM via pyproj
5. Compare to survey coordinates

This makes rmse.py consistent with ODM's orthophoto georeferencing. The residuals should then match the visual offset seen in the ortho report.

**Validation:** After fix, generate rmse_visual_report and verify that dH approximately matches the visual offset between crosshair and target center in the orthophoto crops.

**Data:** aztec6 reconstruction + aztec7 gcp/chk lists + ortho COG for visual comparison.
close_reason: Proper geodetic conversion (ENU→ECEF→LLA→proj) replaces ref+offset + similarity fit. GCP RMS_H=0.025ft, CHK RMS_H=0.114ft.

title: Collect and organize all project data into S3
Flight path planning (placeholder)
description:
Placeholder for future work on optimal flight path planning, to be expanded into sub-beads in a later session.

PRIMARY DESIGN PRINCIPLE: Target RMSE is the primary user input. Everything else (altitude, overlap, image count, flight time) is derived from it.

The RMSE → altitude chain:
  Target RMSE → required GSD (RMSE ≈ 1-2× GSD)
  GSD = (altitude × sensor_width_mm) / (focal_length_mm × image_width_px)
  → altitude = GSD × focal_length × image_width / sensor_width
This chain should be computed by the planning tool given camera model and RMSE target.

USER GOALS AND CONSTRAINTS (planning tool should ask for all of these):
  - Target vertical RMSE (primary — hard constraint or best-effort)
  - Target horizontal RMSE (typically 2× vertical; often secondary)
  - Camera model (determines sensor/focal specs for GSD calc)
  - Max flight time per session (battery count × endurance)
  - Total acceptable flight time for the job
  - Max image count (drives upload time and processing cost)
  - Processing software: WebODM (free) vs. Pix4D ($/image) — affects cost estimate
  - Processing budget (if Pix4D)

DERIVED OUTPUTS:
  - Recommended altitude AGL
  - Recommended front/side overlap
  - Estimated image count
  - Estimated flight time (and battery swap count)
  - Estimated processing cost (WebODM: /bin/zsh; Pix4D: $/image × count)
  - GCP count and spacing recommendation (feeds into GCP placement advisor)

OPTIMIZATION FRAMING:
  Minimize: flight time (or cost)
  Subject to: RMSE ≤ target, images per GCP ≥ 7, corridor fully covered
  Variables: altitude, front overlap, side overlap, GCP count

KEY TRADE-OFF: Hitting the RMSE target at low altitude may require multiple battery
swaps and a high image count (long upload / processing). The tool should surface this
trade-off explicitly so the user can decide whether to relax the RMSE target or
accept the cost.

PLANNED FEATURES (to be broken into sub-beads):
  - Varying gimbal angles: oblique passes over areas of significant Z variation
    provide better vertical accuracy via parallax; tradeoff is more images and
    processing time
  - Adaptive overlap: higher overlap in high-relief or dense-canopy areas
  - Multi-pass strategies: nadir pass for coverage + oblique pass for relief
  - Interaction with GCP placement advisor: flight altitude feeds into geo-5fh
    (coverage filter); oblique passes may shift optimal GCP locations

Related: GCP placement advisor (geo-sge), coverage filter (geo-5fh), WebODM UI (geo-9dk).

title: GCPEditorPro: adaptive zoom crop from marker_bbox column
description:
Read the optional marker_bbox column (col 9, 'x1,y1,x2,y2') produced by the
pixel refinement step (geo-56c) and use it to drive an adaptive zoom crop in
SubImageCropComponent, replacing the current fixed 256x256 window centered on
(imX, imY).

## Problem
SubImageCropComponent currently draws a fixed CROP=256 square centered on the
GCP pixel estimate. This is arbitrary: too small when the marker is large (clips
arms), too large when the marker is small (buries it in terrain context).

## Solution
When marker_bbox is present for an ImageGcp, compute the source crop as:
  1. Parse "x1,y1,x2,y2" integers from marker_bbox.
  2. Compute bbox dimensions: w = x2-x1, h = y2-y1.
  3. Expand each edge by 10% of max(w,h) for display margin.
  4. Center the padded rectangle on the refined (imX, imY) estimate.
  5. Clamp to image bounds.
  6. Scale to fill the 256x256 canvas (aspect ratio preserved; letterbox if needed).
  7. Draw crosshair at (imX, imY) within the scaled view.

Fallback: when marker_bbox is absent, use current behavior (256px centered on
imX/imY). Fully backward compatible with hand-tagged and pre-refinement files.

## Files to change

### gcps-utils.service.ts
- Add optional `markerBbox?: {x1,y1,x2,y2}` field to ImageGcp interface.
- Parse col 9 from gcp_list.txt rows that have >= 9 tab-separated fields.

### sub-image-crop.component.ts
- Add @Input() markerBbox: {x1,y1,x2,y2} | null = null.
- In _draw(): when markerBbox present, compute padded source rect and scale
  to canvas instead of the fixed half=CROP/2 approach.
- The click handler (onCanvasClick) must also be updated to map canvas
  click coords back to full-image coords through the new transform.

### images-tagger.component.html
- Pass [markerBbox]="desc.image.markerBbox" to <app-sub-image-crop>.

## Related
- geo-56c — produces marker_bbox column (must be implemented first)
- docs/gcp_object_identification.md Stage 3e — column spec

Panzoom transform-origin issue fixed (Tx formula).

title: EC2 spot pipeline: automated ODM on spot instances
description:
Rewrote infra/ec2/main.tf to support a fully automated spot-instance ODM pipeline.

## What was built

### Spot instance (persistent + stop-on-interruption)
- instance_market_options with spot_instance_type=persistent, instance_interruption_behavior=stop
- EBS survives interruption; AWS auto-restarts the instance (persistent spot request)
- 500 GB gp3 EBS (300 GB caused disk-full at medium quality)
- --optimize-disk-space removed: it deletes undistorted images after texturing, breaking orthophoto resume

### S3 project layout convention
  s3://stratus-jrstear/PROJECT/
    images/          input images (upload before apply)
    gcp_list.txt     GCP file (upload before apply)
    opensfm/ odm_*/  ODM outputs (written on completion)
- PROJECT is a var, e.g. bsn/aztec or bsn/red-rocks
- S3 mirrors /data/project/ on EBS (flat, no odm_project/ wrapper)

### odm-bootstrap.sh (auto-runs on every boot via @reboot cron)
- .odm-complete marker: exits immediately if pipeline already done (handles persistent-spot auto-restarts after completion)
- New instance: aws s3 sync s3://BUCKET/PROJECT/ /data/project/ (restores images + any prior stage outputs for new-instance resume)
- Spot resume: images already on EBS, skips S3 sync entirely
- Calls odm-run.sh; on success: syncs outputs to S3, sends SNS completion notification

### odm-run.sh (per-stage thread optimization)
- Runs each ODM stage individually via --rerun <stage>
- is_done() checks stage-specific output markers; skips completed stages (safe to call repeatedly)
- is_done(opensfm) also checks for undistorted images — absent images (from --optimize-disk-space on old runs) forces opensfm to rerun
- Per-stage --max-concurrency: 16 (dataset/opensfm/filterpoints/georef/dem/report), 8 (openmvs/meshing/texturing), 4 (orthophoto)
- SNS notification on each stage completion and on failure

### SNS alerts
- aws_sns_topic geo-odm-alerts + email/SMS subscriptions (optional vars)
- EventBridge rules: spot interruption warning (2-min notice) + instance stopped/terminated
- spot-watcher.sh polls IMDS termination endpoint every 5s; sends SNS on interruption

### terraform output
- usage output: static instructions with var.* interpolation — available any time after apply
- instructions output: post-apply instance-specific values (IP, instance ID, SNS ARN, restart command)
- File header comment: full usage readable via head -60 main.tf (no state needed)

### Workflow
  terraform apply -var='project=bsn/aztec' -var='notify_email=...' [-var='notify_phone=...']
  # Everything else is automatic. terraform destroy when done.
notes:
FUTURE WORK: automatic thread downstepping on OOM in odm-run.sh.

If a stage fails (docker exits non-zero), the failure may be OOM. Current behavior: send failure SNS, exit. Desired behavior: detect likely OOM, halve --max-concurrency, retry the stage. Then if it fails again at reduced threads, give up and notify.

Design sketch:
- After non-zero exit, check available memory or docker exit code to guess OOM vs other failure
- Alternatively: always retry with half threads on any failure (simpler; occasional false retries are harmless)
- threads_for() already returns per-stage values; halving would be e.g. max(1, threads/2)
- Could retry once (half), then fail — or retry twice (half, quarter)
- Need to be careful not to infinite-loop on persistent non-OOM failures

Simpler alternative: just retry once at half threads unconditionally on any stage failure, then give up.

title: Study: GCP mis-tag detection via reprojection spread and ODM pixel residuals
description:
Per-image triangulation spread for GCP/CHK mis-tag detection.

For each target tagged in M images, triangulate a 3D position from each image pair. A clean tag produces a tight 3D cluster; a mis-tag on one image produces high spread. This catches per-IMAGE errors that the overall centroid-based dH misses.

Metric: std dev of per-image triangulated positions from the centroid.

This is the one mis-tag detection signal that current rmse.py doesn't provide — rmse.py reports centroid-vs-survey residuals, but a single bad image tag can hide in the centroid average while producing detectable spread.

**Implementation:** Add --spread flag to rmse.py. For each target, compute per-image ray-centroid distance and report max/std. Flag targets with high spread.

**Dependency on geo-e3m removed** — LOO approach superseded by geodetic rmse fix.

**Data:** aztec7 reconstruction + gcp_list.txt + chk_list.txt

title: Post-processing: reproject point cloud/DTM/contours with proper geodetic conversion
description:
ODM's export_geocoords uses a linearized affine approximation (4-point transform) to convert from topocentric ENU to projected CRS. This introduces position-dependent error that grows with distance from the reference point (~20m/km at sites far from the UTM central meridian, e.g. aztec at -107.9° vs CM -105°).

This error affects ALL ODM outputs georeferenced via export_geocoords:
- Point cloud (.laz)
- DTM/DSM rasters
- Contours derived from DTM
- TIN mesh

The true ortho fixes the orthophoto by reprojecting from scratch with proper geodetic math. This bead does the same for the point cloud and derived products.

**Approach:**
1. Read georeferenced .laz point cloud (in linearized UTM coords)
2. Convert back to topocentric ENU (subtract offset from export_geocoords)
3. Convert ENU → ECEF → lat/lon → UTM using proper geodetic conversion
4. Write corrected .laz
5. Regenerate DTM/contours from corrected cloud

**Dependencies:** Need to verify the magnitude of the linearization error on aztec data first. If it's sub-cm, this may not be worth doing.

**Tools:** PDAL for .laz I/O, pyproj for geodetic conversion.
notes:
Before implementing: measure the actual affine approximation error on aztec7 data.

export_geocoords uses a 4-point affine, NOT raw ref+offset. The affine captures first-order grid convergence at the reference point. The residual error (second-order: scale factor variation, convergence angle variation across the site) may be sub-cm, in which case point cloud reprojection is unnecessary.

Test (after aztec7 completes):
1. Pick several points across the corridor (near reference, mid, far)
2. Compute position via affine (what export_geocoords does)
3. Compute position via proper geodetic conversion (what rmse.py does)
4. Measure the difference — this is the actual deliverable error from the linearization

If sub-cm: close this bead as unnecessary.
If > 1cm: implement point cloud reprojection, regenerate DTM/DSM/contours from corrected cloud.
