title: emlid2gcp: demote near-duplicate GCPs to end of list
description:
## Problem
emlid2gcp.py can produce a gcp_list.txt where two GCPs are at essentially the same physical location (e.g. GCPs 27 and 90 in ghostrider gulch are within 15mm of each other — same physical target measured twice under different names). When both land in the top-7, one labeled map pin completely covers the other.

## Solution
As the FIRST step of GCP ordering in emlid2gcp.py:
1. Identify GCPs within a configurable spatial tolerance of each other (default TBD, e.g. 1m or 0.5m).
2. For each near-duplicate group, keep only the first (by original file order) in the main sorting bucket; move the rest to a "demoted" bucket.
3. After the full ordering algorithm runs on the main bucket, append the demoted near-duplicates at the end (in original-file order).

## CLI flags
- `--dup-tolerance METERS` (default: e.g. 1.0) — spatial threshold for near-duplicate detection. Set to 0 to disable.
- `--omit-duplicates` — when set, omit near-duplicate GCPs from the output gcp_list.txt entirely (instead of demoting them to the end).

## Notes
- Tolerance should be in the same units as the coordinate system (projected, meters or feet). Convert to meters internally for the threshold comparison.
- Log/warn which GCPs were demoted or omitted.
- The 15mm case (GCPs 27 and 90) is sub-centimeter — even a 0.1m default tolerance would catch it.
- EPSG:6529 is in ftUS, so tolerance conversion is needed.
close_reason: Implemented _separate_near_duplicates() with 1m default tolerance. Demotes co-located GCPs to end of sorted list. Fixes GCP 27/90 map collision. 25 near-duplicates detected in ghostrider gulch data.

title: odm-run.sh: optional pause after opensfm RMSE for operator go/no-go decision
description:
Add a configurable pause point after the opensfm+RMSE stage: the pipeline can either wait for explicit operator approval before continuing to expensive MVS/meshing/ortho stages, or auto-continue (default).

**Motivation**: opensfm + RMSE takes ~2-4 hrs and reveals reconstruction quality. If accuracy is inadequate (wrong GCP placement, missing check points, bad flags), stopping here saves 15+ hrs of compute on a run that will need to be redone.

**Two modes** (set via Terraform variable or S3 config file):

**Mode A — auto-continue (default)**:
- After opensfm completes and RMSE notification is sent, pipeline immediately proceeds to openmvs
- User can SSH in and kill the run themselves if they decide to restart
- Matches current behavior; no new mechanism needed

**Mode B — pause-for-approval**:
- After RMSE notification, pipeline writes a sentinel file: /data/project/opensfm/WAITING_FOR_APPROVAL
- Polls every 5 min for either: /data/project/opensfm/APPROVED or /data/project/opensfm/ABORT
- SNS notification instructs user: 'To continue: aws s3 cp /dev/null s3://{bucket}/{project}/opensfm/APPROVED --profile personal'
  (or create the file via console/CLI); 'To abort: ...ABORT'
- Timeout: if no response within N hours (configurable, default 24h), auto-continues (prevents indefinite instance idle cost)
- On ABORT sentinel: pipeline exits cleanly, instance shuts down, sends SNS confirmation

**Terraform variable**:
  variable "rmse_pause" { default = false }  # true = Mode B
  Written to /etc/odm-env as RMSE_PAUSE=true/false

**Implementation in odm-run.sh**:
- After geo-6sm RMSE step runs, check RMSE_PAUSE env var
- If true: write WAITING_FOR_APPROVAL, enter poll loop
- Poll loop checks S3 for sentinel files (aws s3 ls s3://{bucket}/{project}/opensfm/APPROVED)
- Log each poll cycle; SNS reminder at 4h if still waiting

**Note on Mode B sentinel approach**: Avoids needing an API endpoint or Lambda; all state is S3 objects. Instance is idle during wait (CPU ~0%) — may trigger CPU watchdog. Watchdog must be suppressed during pause (set IDLE_CPU_THRESHOLD=0 or skip watchdog during poll).

**Dependency**: geo-6sm (RMSE-in-pipeline) must be implemented first.

**Default = auto-continue** (Mode A) so existing behavior is unchanged until operator opts in.
notes: Elevated to P1: spot interruptions during reconstruction cause cascading failures requiring manual intervention. The pause gate should also prevent re-running expensive stages unnecessarily.

title: Grafana: cost-weighted resource dashboard + per-stage/substage breakdown
description:
Build a Grafana dashboard that annotates raw resource metrics with ODM stage/substage boundaries and overlays instance cost rate, producing cost-weighted views that directly answer 'which stages justify a more expensive instance?'

**Stage/substage annotations**:
- odm-run.sh already logs timestamps at each stage start/end — add Grafana annotation API calls (POST /api/annotations) at each boundary
- Within opensfm: tail the docker container log in background; grep for substage markers and push annotations:
  - 'Running feature detectors' → detect_features start
  - 'Matching features' → match_features start  
  - 'Reconstructing' → reconstruct start
  - 'Undistorting' → undistort start
  These substages have very different GPU/CPU/memory profiles — GPU helps detect_features and possibly match_features, not reconstruct.

**Cost overlay**:
- Terraform writes INSTANCE_COST_PER_HOUR to /etc/odm-env (e.g. 0.768 for m5.4xlarge on-demand, 0.23 for spot)
- node_exporter textfile collector exposes it as a gauge: odm_instance_cost_per_hour
- Grafana derived metrics:
  - cost_rate × memory_%  = 'memory cost efficiency'
  - cost_rate × cpu_%     = 'cpu cost efficiency'
  - Integrate over stage duration → cost_per_stage (dollars)
- Panel: stacked bar of cost_per_stage — immediately shows where the money goes

**Dashboard panels** (suggested):
1. Memory % over time, annotated with stage/substage boundaries
2. CPU % per core over time (reveals single-threaded bottlenecks in reconstruction)
3. Disk read/write throughput (I/O-bound stages: dataset, S3 sync)
4. Net throughput (S3 upload at stage end)
5. Cost per stage (bar chart, integrated from cost_rate × wall_time)
6. Memory headroom (MemAvailable — critical: if <2GB, OOM imminent)
7. Repeat for multiple runs overlaid (job label selector variable)

**Instance selection guidance** (what this data enables):
- detect_features: GPU-accelerated in opensfm if CUDA present; memory moderate
- match_features: CPU-parallel, memory spikes with large image sets (aztec OOMs here?)
- reconstruct/bundle_adjust: single-threaded CPU; memory proportional to track count
- openmvs: GPU strongly preferred; memory ~16-32GB
- odm_meshing: RAM-heavy CPU; 64-128GB needed at Red Rocks scale
- Actual measurements may contradict conventional wisdom — that's the point

**Dashboard as code**: export JSON and commit to infra/grafana/ so it can be re-imported after free-tier purge or workspace change.

**Dependency**: geo-250 (telemetry infrastructure) must be deployed first to have data to visualize.
**Informs**: geo-8fg (stage switching — use measured profiles to justify/size instance changes)
notes:
Live pricing implemented (2026-03-28):
- odm-bootstrap.sh: lookup_instance_cost() queries IMDS for lifecycle (spot/on-demand), then spot describe-spot-instance-requests or Pricing API for current on-demand rate. Writes INSTANCE_COST_PER_HOUR to /etc/odm-env.
- odm-progress.sh: writes odm_instance_cost_per_hour{project} metric to textfile collector.
- IAM: added pricing:GetProducts + ec2:DescribeSpotInstanceRequests to role.
- Dashboard: panels 9 (cost rate stat), 10 (stage cost stat), 11 (cost accrual timeseries) added to odm-pipeline.json.

title: docs: coordinate system flow guide — explicit CRS at every pipeline handoff
description:
Document the coordinate system in use at every step of the four-stage workflow, so any team member can correctly load data in QGIS, configure Emlid, and hand off deliverables without guessing.

## The four stages

**1. Receive from customer (BSN .dc file)**
- .dc file stores control monuments in design-grid CRS (raw DC coords; northing first, easting second; offset ~1.5M ft from state plane)
- Base stations (66KI/66FD) stored as lat/lon
- Run extract_dc_points.py → F100340_{job}_points.csv in EPSG:3618 (state plane ft)
- This CSV is the ground truth for all subsequent steps

**2. Survey and fly**
- Emlid localized to F100340_{job}_points.csv (EPSG:3618 state plane ft)
- Emlid outputs Easting/Northing in EPSG:6529 (NAD83(2011) NM Central ftUS) ≈ EPSG:3618
- Load in QGIS: X=Easting, Y=Northing, CRS=EPSG:6529 (NOT Longitude/Latitude columns)
- Emlid Longitude/Latitude columns are WGS84 degrees — wrong column for QGIS state plane overlay
- GCPEditorPro input: confirmed.txt header specifies EPSG:6529

**3. Process and QC (ODM + rmse_calc)**
- convert_coords.py: confirmed.txt (EPSG:6529) → gcp_list.txt + chk_list.txt (EPSG:32613 metres)
- ODM runs in EPSG:32613 (UTM 13N) — all axes in metres, no ambiguous unit conversion
- ODM output orthophoto: EPSG:32613
- reproject_deliverable.py: EPSG:32613 → EPSG:3618 for QGIS review
- Load in QGIS: ortho (EPSG:3618) + F100340_points.csv (EPSG:6529 ≈ EPSG:3618) → should overlap exactly

**4. Deliver to customer**
- Ortho in EPSG:3618 → package.py --shift-x +1546702.929 --shift-y -3567.471 → design grid
- Vector layers (contours, TIN): same shift applied
- Document design-grid offset for client's CAD software

## Why EPSG:6529 ≠ 'design grid'
EPSG:6529 is the true NAD83(2011) state plane. The design grid is EPSG:3618 + a ~1.5M ft translation. These are NOT the same. All internal processing uses state plane. Design grid appears only in deliverable step 4.

## Common QGIS mistakes to document
- Loading Emlid CSV with X=Longitude, Y=Latitude (degrees) instead of X=Easting, Y=Northing (ft) → points land ~1.5M ft from ortho
- Loading any layer with wrong CRS 'assigned' instead of 'reprojected' → silent misalignment
- Using odm_orthophoto.tif (stub, 429KB) instead of odm_orthophoto.original.tif (1.1GB, real data)

## Output
Extend docs/odm-workflow.md with a CRS table showing the CRS at each handoff, or create docs/coordinate-flow.md. Reference from the workflow diagram.

title: Experiment driver: orchestrate ODM run matrix + collect results
description: Implement GCPSighter/experiment_driver.py. Defines experiment matrix (parametrize: control_label_sets, images_per_label [3,5,7,9,all], nadir_weight [0.0,0.2,1.0]). For each config: (1) calls experiment_gen.py to write gcp_experiment_N.txt, (2) uploads to S3, (3) submits AWS Batch odm-run job, (4) waits for completion (poll batch describe-jobs), (5) submits rmse-calc job as dependent, (6) collects rmse_report.json from S3. After all runs: writes results_table.csv (one row per run: config params + RMS_X, RMS_Y, RMS_Z, RMS_3D, N_chk). CLI: python experiment_driver.py --project ghostrider-gulch --matrix configs/matrix.json --out results_table.csv. Full spec: docs/experiment-framework-spec.md + docs/cloud-infra-spec.md. Acceptance: 3-run smoke test with small image subset completes end-to-end; results_table.csv populated correctly.
notes:
CRITICAL OPTIMIZATION (2026-03-12): For ablation RMSE experiments, dense reconstruction (MVS), meshing, texturing, DEM, and orthophoto are ALL unnecessary. RMSE is determined by sparse SfM + bundle adjustment only. Skipping dense saves 10-20x per run.

IMPLEMENTATION APPROACHES (verify flags against odm:3.3.0):

Option A — OpenSfM direct (fastest, ~20-45 min/run for 1393 imgs on m5.4xlarge):
  Run ODM dataset stage first (sets up opensfm/config.yaml with GCP path, camera priors):
    docker run --rm -v /data/project:/datasets/project opendronemap/odm:3.3.0       --project-path /datasets project --rerun-from dataset --end-with dataset
  Then run OpenSfM sparse reconstruction only:
    docker run --rm --entrypoint bash -v /data/project:/datasets/project opendronemap/odm:3.3.0       -c 'cd /datasets/project && opensfm detect_features opensfm && opensfm match_features opensfm && opensfm create_tracks opensfm && opensfm reconstruct opensfm'
  reconstruction.json path: /datasets/project/opensfm/reconstruction.json (NOT odm_opensfm/)

Option B — ODM minimal flags (simpler, ~1.5-2 hr/run, still runs lowest-quality dense):
    docker run --rm -v /data/project:/datasets/project opendronemap/odm:3.3.0       --project-path /datasets project --pc-quality lowest --skip-3dmodel --skip-report --orthophoto-resolution 100
  reconstruction.json path: /datasets/project/odm_opensfm/reconstruction.json

Option A is strongly preferred for cost. Option B is fallback if --end-with is not available in 3.3.0.

IMAGE SUBSETS: Using all images is correct (connectivity required for SfM). Do NOT attempt to subset images or black out non-GCP regions — this breaks feature matching chains. The speedup from sparse-only already dominates.

COST IMPACT: Option A reduces per-run cost from ~/bin/zsh.60-0.80 (full ODM medium) to ~/bin/zsh.05-0.10. Full 50-run ablation: ~-5 instead of ~-50.

run.sh in cloud-infra-spec.md needs to be updated to implement Option A. See experiment-framework-spec.md Pipeline Optimization section for full details.

title: Study: GCP mis-tag detection via leave-one-out residuals
description:
GCP mis-tagging is harder to detect than CHK because bundle adjustment warps the reconstruction to fit GCPs, suppressing in-sample residuals. CHK outlier flagging (5× median dH) cannot be directly applied to GCPs.

**Approach: Leave-One-Out (LOO) residuals**
For each GCP i, refit the similarity transform from the remaining N-1 GCPs, then compute GCP i residual against that out-of-sample transform. A mis-tagged GCP will have a large LOO residual even if its in-sample residual looks fine.

All required data is already available from rmse.py internals (triangulated 3D coords + survey coords) — no ODM re-run needed.

**Study design:**
1. Compute LOO residuals for all GCPs using aztec7 data (gcp_list.txt + aztec6 reconstruction)
2. Compare LOO dH vs in-sample dH per GCP — quantify how much the transform suppresses bad tags
3. Inject a synthetic mis-tag (shift one GCP's survey coord by e.g. 1 ft, 5 ft, 10 ft) and measure:
   - How much does LOO residual change?
   - How much does in-sample residual change?
   - At what mis-tag magnitude does LOO reliably flag it?
4. Also compute per-image triangulation spread per GCP (std dev of per-image 3D positions) as a secondary signal
5. Define a flagging threshold analogous to CHK: flag if LOO dH > N× median LOO dH

**Expected outputs:**
- accuracy_study/gcp_loo_study.py — study script
- Recommendation: threshold value and whether LOO alone is sufficient or spread is needed too
- If validated: add LOO flagging to rmse.py (separate bead for implementation)

**Data:**
- Reconstruction: ~/stratus/aztec6/opensfm/reconstruction.topocentric.json
- GCP list: ~/stratus/aztec7/gcp_list.txt (10 GCPs)
- rmse.py already fits similarity transform in _fit_similarity() — reuse that
close_reason: Largely resolved: with proper geodetic conversion (no similarity transform), GCP residuals are already independent — no fitted transform to overfit. The 5× median outlier flagging in rmse.py catches mis-tags for both GCP and CHK. LOO adds marginal value without the similarity. Remaining per-image spread signal → geo-aw0.

title: Design: coordinate conversion timing — evaluate moving CRS transform earlier in pipeline
description:
Currently the CRS conversion (survey/field CRS → EPSG:32613 for ODM) happens at the last moment in convert_coords.py, just before the ODM run. Evaluate whether converting earlier would simplify the pipeline overall.

**Current flow**:
  field survey (EPSG:6529 ft) → CSV → sight.py (uses EPSG:6529 for projection) → GCPEditorPro (shows EPSG:6529 coords) → convert_coords.py → gcp_list.txt (EPSG:32613 m) → ODM

**Earlier-conversion alternative**:
  field survey (EPSG:6529 ft) → CSV → convert to EPSG:32613 → sight.py (projects in UTM) → GCPEditorPro (shows UTM) → gcp_list.txt already in EPSG:32613 → ODM (no convert_coords.py needed)

**Arguments for earlier conversion**:
- Everything downstream (sight.py projections, rmse_calc, deliverables) works in one CRS — less mental overhead
- convert_coords.py step removed from pre-ODM workflow
- rmse_calc already works in EPSG:32613; no impedance
- geo-zjq (cameras.json Brown model) projections also work in whatever CRS sight.py uses

**Arguments for later conversion (current approach)**:
- GCPEditorPro shows state-plane coordinates the field crew recognizes (EPSG:6529) — useful sanity check while tagging
- Pix4D parallel workflow (marks.csv) uses EPSG:6529 — converting early would require a separate marks.csv in field CRS
- Field surveyor delivers in EPSG:6529; preserving that through tagging keeps the 'raw' data in native CRS
- Easier to audit: confirmed.txt is in the same CRS as the Emlid CSV

**Key question for Pix4D compatibility**: GCPEditorPro currently exports in whatever CRS the input file uses. If we want GCPEditorPro exports to be usable directly in both ODM (EPSG:32613) and Pix4D (EPSG:6529/3618), is a single earlier-conversion the right answer, or should convert_coords.py remain as a format adapter?

**Recommendation to evaluate**: possibly a hybrid — convert to EPSG:32613 for sight.py (better Brown-model projections), but keep a field-CRS export for Pix4D compatibility. Or: convert_coords.py stays but becomes trivial (just a header rewrite, no unit conversion, since units already match).

**Output**: a decision (with rationale) recorded in docs/coordinate-design.md and any pipeline changes that follow from it.
close_reason: Resolved by transform.py pipeline: dc outputs field-CRS CSVs, sight.py converts to EPSG:32613 via transform.yaml, split produces gcp_list.txt in EPSG:32613. No separate convert_coords.py step needed. The hybrid approach was naturally adopted.

title: Job transform config: capture forward+inverse CRS transform in a per-job file
description:
The coordinate transform for a BSN job has two separable components: (1) CRS reprojection (EPSG:32613 → EPSG:3618, nonlinear, requires gdalwarp pixel resampling), (2) design-grid Helmert transform (for aztec3: pure translation, scale=1.0, rotation=0°; may differ for other jobs — derived from tie points in .dc file).

Capture both in a per-job transform.yaml so that: the full pipeline is reproducible, package.py/app.py can load it to auto-populate all fields, and the inverse is available for deliverable generation.

**Proposed transform.yaml** (~/{job}/transform.yaml):
  job: aztec3
  odm_crs: EPSG:32613            # ODM output CRS
  field_crs: EPSG:3618           # state plane (for QGIS review, Emlid, Pix4D)
  helmert:                       # design-grid transform (state plane → design grid)
    shift_x_ft: +1546702.929     # matches package.py --shift-x
    shift_y_ft: -3567.471        # matches package.py --shift-y
    scale: 1.0                   # from Helmert fit (aztec3: translation-only)
    rotation_deg: 0.0            # from Helmert fit (aztec3: no rotation)
    anchor_x_ft: 0.0             # anchor for scale/rotation (package.py --anchor-x)
    anchor_y_ft: 0.0
  notes: 'F100340 Aztec Highway. Helmert from helmert_from_tiepoints.py, 18 tie pairs, residuals <0.001 ft.'

**package.py / app.py integration** (primary deliverable of this bead):
- package.py gains --transform-yaml flag: reads yaml, runs gdalwarp reproject step (odm_crs → field_crs) then applies helmert shift/scale/rotation via VRT — all in one command
- app.py gains 'Load transform.yaml' button: populates target CRS field, shift_x/y, scale, anchor fields automatically; user can still override individual fields
- Reproject step uses gdal.Warp() (in-process, no temp file) rather than subprocess
- For the aztec3 case: scale=1.0 and rotation=0° so the VRT geotransform is shift-only, same as current package.py behavior

**Why per-job**: Helmert parameters are BSN job-specific (derived from .dc tie points). CRS components (32613→3618) are the same for all NM Central jobs. The yaml separates job-specific from generalizable.

**Auto-derivation pipeline** (when geo-ff7 dc2csv is complete):
  dc2csv --dc F100340_AZTEC.dc → F100340_AZTEC_points.csv + transform.yaml
  helmert_from_tiepoints.py validates and confirms parameters

**Dependency**: geo-ff7 (dc2csv generalization) — dc2csv writes transform.yaml as a byproduct, making the whole chain reproducible from the .dc file alone.
notes: transform.yaml schema finalized: job, field_crs, odm_crs, delivery_crs, design_grid (shift_x/y, scale, rotation_deg, helmert_residual_ft, anchor_x/y). Written by transformer.py dc, read by transformer.py split. package.py --transform-yaml integration is the eta scope.

description:

- 71KI/E2BA/E2SI/73BA: Trimble housekeeping — ignore

**Helmert auto-derivation**: match 69KI point IDs against 66-derived base points (same physical monuments appear twice — once as design-grid 69KI, once as lat/lon 66KI). Fit translation. For aztec: scale=1.0, rotation=0° confirmed.

- TargetSighter/reproject_deliverable.py: ODM ortho (EPSG:32613) → EPSG:3618 (gdalwarp wrapper)

- docs/coordinate-flow.md: full CRS reference guide (committed 291ecbf)

title: Zoom view: unified click-to-pin + hover-to-preview
description:

title: Add non-nadir image detection and warning in Mode A projection
description:
gcp_pipeline.py Mode A (project_pixel_mode_a) assumes nadir imagery (camera pointing straight down, pitch ≈ -90°). This is baked into the math — the gimbal_pitch EXIF field is read but never used in the projection.

If given oblique images (e.g. from a handheld camera, or a drone in non-nadir mission mode), Mode A will silently produce wrong pixel estimates with no warning.

Work to do:
1. In project_pixel_mode_a(): check gimbal_pitch if available.
   If abs(gimbal_pitch - (-90.0)) > NADIR_TOLERANCE_DEG (suggest 15°), skip the image
   and return None with a logged warning, OR add a 'nadir_warn' flag to the return.

2. In _footprint_worker() / match_images_to_gcps(): if pitch is non-nadir,
   either skip the image or use a conservative fallback radius (footprint shape is
   wrong for oblique).

3. In run_pipeline(): collect and summarize non-nadir images in a warning message
   at the end (e.g. '3 images skipped: non-nadir pitch (Mode A only handles nadir)').

4. Mode B (project_pixel_mode_b) is unaffected — it uses the full rotation matrix
   from reconstruction.json and handles any orientation.

5. Add a --allow-oblique flag to the CLI to suppress the skip behavior if user
   explicitly wants to try oblique images anyway.

Design note: NADIR_TOLERANCE_DEG = 15 means any pitch outside [-105°, -75°] is
flagged. DJI drones in nadir mode typically report -90°.
notes: gimbal_pitch is read in EXIF batch but never used in projection math. Oblique images produce silently wrong results.
close_reason: Implemented full 3D rotation matrix (pitch+yaw) in project_pixel_mode_a() — handles both nadir and oblique correctly. Nadir case is mathematically identical to old formula. Added --nadir-only flag to skip oblique images when desired. Commits a60f630 and c0804a7.

title: Ortho uncertainty overlay: per-pixel positional confidence from camera geometry + DTM
description:
Generate a GeoTIFF overlay showing estimated horizontal positional uncertainty at each orthophoto pixel.

**Concept:**
For each ortho pixel, the positional uncertainty is approximately:
  σ_horizontal ≈ σ_DTM(x,y) × tan(θ_off_nadir) + σ_reconstruction

Where:
- σ_DTM = DTM uncertainty at that XY (function of point cloud density, interpolation distance, ground classification confidence)
- θ_off_nadir = viewing angle of the camera selected for that pixel
- σ_reconstruction = base reconstruction accuracy (from rmse.py CHK RMS_H, ~0.1 ft)

**Inputs available:**
- reconstruction.topocentric.json: camera positions + orientations
- DTM/DSM GeoTIFF: surface model with implicit quality info
- Dense point cloud: can compute local point density and ground classification stats
- Orthophoto: defines the output grid

**Output:** Single-band GeoTIFF (same extent/resolution as ortho) with σ_horizontal in metres per pixel. Can be styled as a heatmap overlay in QGIS.

**Prior art:** Pix4D 'quality map', academic displacement/uncertainty maps. Standard in survey photogrammetry but absent from ODM.

**Dependencies:** Needs a way to determine which camera ODM selected per ortho pixel (may require parsing ODM internals or recomputing from camera positions + mesh visibility).

title: Threaded email notifications via SES instead of SNS
description:
SNS email notifications don't thread in Apple Mail (or most clients) because SNS sends each message independently with no RFC 2822 threading headers (In-Reply-To, References).

**Approach:** Replace `aws sns publish` calls in odm-run.sh and odm-bootstrap.sh with `aws ses send-raw-email` that includes threading headers.

**Implementation:**
1. First notification per job: generate a UUID, write to /data/project/.email-thread-id, use as Message-ID
2. All subsequent notifications: include In-Reply-To and References headers pointing to the first Message-ID
3. Helper script on EC2 (e.g. /usr/local/bin/odm-notify.sh) wraps the raw email construction
4. SES requires domain/email verification — verify jrstear@gmail.com in SES console (one-time)
5. IAM: add ses:SendRawEmail to the EC2 role

**No Lambda needed** — the EC2 instance sends emails directly via SES. The thread-id file persists on EBS across spot interruptions.

**SNS retained for:** Grafana annotations, non-email alerts (SMS), and as a fanout mechanism if needed later.

title: ODM upstream: fix export_geocoords linearization error
description:
ODM's export_geocoords (OpenSFM opensfm/actions/export_geocoords.py) uses a 4-point linearized affine to convert topocentric → projected CRS. This is exact at the reference point but introduces position-dependent error from UTM grid convergence and scale factor variation.

At sites far from the UTM central meridian (e.g. 3° offset), the error is ~20m/km, affecting all georeferenced outputs: point cloud, DTM, DSM, orthophoto, contours.

The fix: replace the 4-point affine with per-point geodetic conversion (TopocentricConverter.to_lla → pyproj projection). More expensive but exact.

**Note on why this hasn't been found:** Most ODM users fly small sites (<500m extent) near sea level, where the linearization error is sub-cm and invisible. Survey-quality work on multi-km corridors (like our aztec job at 3° from CM) exposes it. Also, most users don't have sub-foot survey control to measure against.

Related: geo-005 (true ortho upstream contribution).

title: Research: multi-cloud provider comparison for ODM compute (CPU/GPU/spot)
description:
Survey cloud providers beyond AWS to understand hardware options, spot/preemptible availability, and pricing for ODM-class workloads. Goal: make cost-vs-convenience tradeoffs explicit rather than defaulting to AWS familiarity.

**Workload profile** (from geo-250/geo-c8z measurements once available; use estimates for now):
- opensfm: 16-32 vCPU, 32-64 GB RAM, GPU helpful for feature detection/matching
- openmvs: GPU strongly preferred (NVIDIA, 16+ GB VRAM), 32 GB RAM
- odm_meshing: 64-128 GB RAM, CPU only (Red Rocks scale)
- Total wall time: ~4-20 hrs depending on image count and instance type

**Providers to evaluate** (at minimum):
- AWS (baseline): m5.4xlarge /bin/zsh.768/hr on-demand, spot ~/bin/zsh.23/hr; g4dn.12xlarge for GPU
- DigitalOcean: Droplets (CPU-optimized), GPU Droplets (H100 1-8x), no spot but predictable pricing
- Hetzner Cloud: Very competitive CPU pricing (CCX series up to 48 vCPU/192 GB); AX series dedicated; EU/US datacenters; no spot
- Lambda Labs: GPU-focused (A100/H100), cheap GPU rates (~.10/hr A100), no spot; primarily ML but Docker-capable
- Vast.ai: GPU spot marketplace, often 5-10× cheaper than AWS for GPU; less reliability guarantee
- Oracle Cloud Free Tier: 4 OCPUs / 24 GB ARM (Ampere A1) always free — useful for small test runs
- Google Cloud: preemptible/spot VMs, TPUs (less relevant), sustained-use discounts
- Azure: spot VMs, NC-series GPU

**For each provider, capture**:
- Relevant instance types and specs (vCPU, RAM, GPU model/VRAM)
- On-demand price ($/hr)
- Spot/preemptible price and interruption model (notice time, reattach EBS equivalent?)
- Persistent block storage: cost $/GB/month, survives instance stop?
- S3-equivalent object storage and egress cost
- Docker support (required — ODM runs in a container)
- Region availability (us-west preferred for latency to ABQ NM)
- Terraform provider maturity
- Any ODM-specific community reports

**Key questions**:
- Does Hetzner/DO offer persistent volumes that survive instance deletion (like EBS)? Critical for stage-resume pattern.
- Vast.ai interruption model: is it compatible with the EBS-resume approach or does it require full S3-based checkpointing?
- Are there providers with dedicated GPU spot cheaper than AWS g4dn (~/bin/zsh.40/hr spot)?
- DigitalOcean GPU Droplets: H100 pricing vs AWS p4/p5?

**Output**: a markdown table in docs/ comparing providers on the above dimensions, with a recommendation section for: (a) cheapest option for CPU-only opensfm runs, (b) cheapest option for GPU openmvs, (c) most operationally simple if Terraform/spot complexity is a concern.

**Note**: Operational overhead matters. AWS is more complex but has mature Terraform, spot fleet, and EBS persistence. A cheaper provider with no spot or no persistent block storage may require more pipeline re-engineering (full S3 checkpointing after every stage). Quantify that tradeoff.

**Informs**: geo-8fg (stage switching — provider choice affects whether per-stage instance changes are practical), geo-250 (telemetry — provider must support Docker + open ports for Grafana Alloy remote_write)

title: Mini study: CHK RMSE vs image count and outlier exclusion
description:
Investigate why CHK RMSE degraded after tagging all images in aztec_tagged.txt.

Baseline (aztec6 reconstruction, aztec7 tagging, all images):
  RMS_H = 6.68 ft, RMS_Z = 1.38 ft — dominated by CHK-14 (dH=34 ft) and CHK-18 (dH=17.6 ft)
  Without outliers, most CHKs < 0.3 ft horizontal.

Hypotheses:
  H1: CHK-14/CHK-18 are monument base-station points with poor camera geometry → exclude them
  H2: Including all images per CHK (even lens-edge distorted ones) worsens accuracy
  H3: rmse.py has a bug

Study design:
  1. Generate chk_list variants: top N images per CHK label (N = 1, 3, 5, 7, all)
  2. Also generate variants excluding CHK-14 and CHK-18 (base station monuments)
  3. Run rmse.py on each variant, compare RMS_H/RMS_Z/RMS_3D
  4. Conclude whether image count limit or outlier exclusion (or both) is the fix

Script: accuracy_study/chk_image_study.py
Inputs: aztec7/chk_list.txt, aztec6/opensfm/reconstruction.topocentric.json
notes:
Study results (aztec6 recon, aztec7 tagging):

all imgs all CHKs:       N=33  RMS_H=6.68 ft  RMS_Z=1.38 ft
top7 excl CHK-14/18:     N=31  RMS_H=0.11 ft  RMS_Z=1.17 ft
all imgs excl CHK-14/18: N=31  RMS_H=0.36 ft  RMS_Z=1.15 ft

Conclusions:
1. CHK-14 mis-tagged (base station != target location, ~34 ft dH) — process error, not systematic
2. CHK-18 also outlier (~17.6 ft dH) — likely same cause (monument vs target confusion)
3. User theory confirmed: more images from distorted lens regions hurts RMS_H (top7=0.11 vs all=0.36 ft)
4. Vertical accuracy (~1.15 ft) independent of image count — characteristic of aztec6 reconstruction

Action items:
- Add outlier flagging to rmse.py: warn when point dH > median_dH * N (e.g. N=5) → SUSPECT TAGGING
- This would have caught CHK-14 immediately at 34 ft vs median ~0.1 ft
- Connects to geo-6sm: rmse review should gate further ODM pipeline stages
close_reason: Outlier flagging implemented in rmse.py (5× median + 0.5 ft floor). Study confirmed CHK-14/18 are process errors and top-7 images beats all images for RMS_H.

title: GCPEditorPro: split confirmed export into control and check point files
description:
Currently gcp_confirmed.txt mixes GCPs (control points, named GCP-*) and check
points (CHK-* prefix) into one file. ODM and other photogrammetric tools require
these to be separate inputs.

## Change

Replace the single "Download gcp_confirmed.txt" button with two buttons:

  "Download gcp_control.txt"  — confirmed rows whose gcpName starts with "GCP-"
  "Download gcp_check.txt"    — confirmed rows whose gcpName starts with "CHK-"

Keep "Download gcp_list.txt" unchanged (all rows, for resume/reload).

## Button coloring

### gcp_control.txt button
Same pipeline-aware coloring as the old confirmed button:
  - Red:   0–2 GCP-* control points with ≥7 confirmed images
  - Amber: 3–6
  - Green: ≥7
Disabled when no confirmed GCP-* rows exist.

### gcp_check.txt button (new, distinct)
Same color thresholds as GCP-* (consistent with the per-GCP badge):
  - Red:   0–2 CHK-* check points with ≥7 confirmed images (or 0 total)
  - Amber: 3–6
  - Green: ≥7
Disabled when no confirmed CHK-* rows exist.

The matching color scheme makes both buttons self-explanatory: both turn green
when "enough" points in that class are sufficiently tagged.

## Files to change

### export-config.component.ts
- Add exportControl() and exportCheck() methods (filter on gcpName prefix).
- Remove or repurpose exportConfirmed().
- Add gcpControlConfirmedCount: confirmed GCP-* rows with ≥7 tagged images.
- Add gcpCheckConfirmedCount:   confirmed CHK-* rows with ≥7 tagged images.
- Add controlBtnClass / checkBtnClass using the shared threshold helper
  (CONFIRMED_GREEN=7, CONFIRMED_AMBER=3 — same as gcps-map and images-tagger).

### export-config.component.html
- Replace single confirmed download button with two side-by-side buttons.
- Each button shows its confirmed-count badge using the same red/amber/green.
- Update the info alert text to explain the three files and their purpose.

### export-config.component.scss (if needed)
- Minor layout for side-by-side buttons.

## UX notes
- If no CHK-* confirmed rows exist, "Download gcp_check.txt" is disabled.
- If no GCP-* confirmed rows exist, "Download gcp_control.txt" is disabled.
- DUP-* rows are excluded from both downloads (near-duplicates; not useful
  as either control or check points).

## Background
ODM has no native check-point concept — all rows it receives are used as
control. The GCP-*/CHK-* split is organisational: control file goes to ODM
for bundle adjustment; check file is used post-processing for independent
RMSE accuracy figures (manual step until geo-ksv follow-on tooling).

title: GCPEditorPro: auto-enable zoom view when gcp_list.txt has confidence column
description:
When a gcp_list.txt produced by emlid2gcp.py is loaded, automatically switch
the images-tagger to zoom view without requiring the user to toggle it manually.

## Detection heuristic
The 8th tab-separated column (index 7) is 'confidence', written by emlid2gcp.py
as 'projection' (or 'reconstruction' for Mode B). Standard hand-tagged GCP files
from GCPEditorPro have only 7 columns (geo_x, geo_y, geo_z, px, py, filename,
gcp_label). The presence of an 8th column is a reliable signal that the file was
pipeline-generated and has pre-estimated pixel positions worth reviewing in zoom view.

Detection should happen at import time (wherever gcp_list.txt is parsed into
storage.imageGcps). Set a flag on StorageService (e.g. storage.hasPipelineEstimates)
when any row has >= 8 columns.

## Behavior
- If hasPipelineEstimates is true when the user enters the images-tagger:
  auto-enable zoom view (set this.zoomView = true, persist to localStorage)
- If hasPipelineEstimates is false (hand-tagged file or no confidence column):
  leave zoom view in whatever state localStorage has (current behavior)
- The user can still toggle zoom view off manually after auto-enable

## Implementation notes
- Parser is likely in gcps-utils.service.ts (ImageGcp / GcpsUtilsService)
- StorageService is in storage.service.ts
- images-tagger.component.ts ngOnInit() is where zoomView is initialised —
  this is where the auto-enable logic goes
- Already reads localStorage: zoomView = localStorage.getItem('zoomView') === 'true'
- Auto-enable should override localStorage when hasPipelineEstimates is true,
  so the user lands in zoom view on first open even if they previously had it off
close_reason: Single line in images-tagger ngOnInit(): if hasPipelineEstimates then zoomView=true, before filterImages() runs async. Does not write to localStorage, preserving user's manual preference for non-pipeline files.

title: GCPEditorPro: confirmed/total progress indicators + top-7 summary

### 1. GCP list view (gcps-map component)

title: Refinement algorithm tuning
description:
Known failure modes and candidate fixes for the Stage 3 pixel refinement
algorithm in _refine_single() / _refine_all_estimates().

## Failure modes

### 1. Oil spot / discolored ground selected over nearby orange X
Motivating case: GCP-13, DJI_20260212133226_0115_V.JPG
mean_dev + coherence multipliers added (commit 8bd1173) but no observed
improvement. Root cause likely: projected seed lands closer to oil spot
than to the X, and the oil spot is larger, so it still wins on
ratio×(λ_min+λ_max)×mean_dev×coherence.
Candidate fix: distance-from-seed soft penalty, e.g. score /= (1 + k*d).
See comment in _refine_single() near the scoring loop.

### 2. Wrong-color object selected (tree, bush) vs correct-color marker
Motivating case: GCP-96 — some images correctly find clay pigeons (orange),
others position on a nearby tree (green) or bush (grey).
Candidate fix: majority-color consensus re-evaluation pass (see below).

### 3. Asymmetric / partially-occluded X rejected by ratio test
_MIN_AXIS_RATIO = 2/3 may be too strict for real markers with one arm
partially occluded or viewed obliquely (ratio can drop to 0.3–0.5).
Candidate fix: lower threshold or make it adaptive.

### 4. High-altitude images: marker below MIN_PIXELS floor
At high AGL the marker may subtend <20 px, causing no refinement.
Candidate fix: scale MIN_PIXELS floor with estimated GSD or AGL.

### 5. Background ring contaminated by marker
If projected seed is far from actual marker, the 55–90 px background ring
may overlap the marker itself, corrupting bg_mean and suppressing
deviation_map for the correct component.

## Majority-color consensus re-evaluation (for failure mode 2)

After _refine_all_estimates() processes all (GCP, image) pairs, add a
per-GCP post-pass:

1. For each successful refinement of a GCP, record the mean LAB a*,b*
   (or HSV hue+sat) of the selected component — the "chosen color".
2. Cluster chosen colors across images for that GCP.  If a clear majority
   color exists (e.g. ≥60% of successful refinements agree on orange),
   treat that as the consensus marker color.
3. Re-run _refine_single() on:
   a. Images where refinement failed entirely.
   b. Images where the chosen component color is an outlier vs consensus
      (e.g. green or grey when most found orange).
   In the re-run, add a tight HSV/LAB color filter centered on the
   consensus color as an additional hard gate in Stage 3a, overriding the
   general anomaly detection.
4. Replace the estimate if the re-run finds a consensus-colored component.

This leverages the physical constraint that a GCP marker is the same
object in every image and should always be the same color.
notes:
## Tuning recommendations (from 85-pair ground truth comparison, 2026-03-04)

### Ground truth results
- 51% of confirmed pairs < 5 px error (sub-pixel accurate)
- 46% > 50 px error (wrong feature entirely)
- Bimodal: algorithm either nails it or completely misses, no middle ground
- Per-GCP: GCP-27 100% success, GCP-13 8% success, others mixed
- Bbox size is strong discriminator: good cases consistent, bad cases wildly variable

### Prioritized recommendations

R1. Distance-from-seed soft penalty (highest impact, simplest)
score /= (1 + k * distance_from_seed), k ~ 0.01-0.02
Encodes prior that projection estimate is usually in right neighborhood.
STATUS: implementing

R2. Bbox size consistency post-pass
Compute median bbox w/h per GCP after pass 1. Revert results whose bbox
deviates > 2x from median back to projection estimate.

R3. Minimum score threshold / fallback to projection
Do not overwrite projection with a 5x5 noise speck. Require minimum absolute
score; below threshold keep projection estimate, confidence=projection.

R4. Majority-color consensus re-pass (already designed in description)
Compute consensus marker color from successful hits, re-run failures with
tight color gate.

R5. Multi-signal post-pass (unified R2+R4)
Per-GCP: compute consensus bbox size, color, score profile. Re-run outliers
(distance, bbox, or color deviating from consensus) with tightened constraints.
This is the global vote idea from user comment.

R6. Skip refinement beyond GSD threshold
High-altitude images where marker < ~15 px are unlikely to refine successfully.
Skip refinement, keep projection estimate.

R7. Self-calibrating re-projection (speculative)
Automatically identify high-confidence (gcp,image) pairs using multiple
signals: closest to projection estimate, most nadir viewing angle, best PCA
ratio, consistent bbox size. Use these high-confidence matches as pseudo-GCPs
to compute correction factors for the drone GPS positions (effectively a
coarse bundle adjustment or affine correction per flight line). Then
re-project all low-confidence pairs using the calibrated camera positions
and re-run refinement with tighter search radii and image-stat priors
(color, bbox size) from the high-confidence matches. May be overkill but
leverages the physical constraint that GPS error is systematic/smooth, not
random per-image.

title: Auto-zoom images to GCP estimate location on load
description:
When an image loads in images-tagger and a GCP pixel estimate exists (pinLocation is set), the view should automatically pan+zoom to show the area around that GCP point. Currently images load showing the full image at 1:1 scale — GCP targets are typically tiny relative to the full image, requiring manual zoom and pan every time.

DESIRED UX:
- Image loads → immediately zoomed in and centered on the estimated GCP pixel
- Default zoom level = show ~10 sq meters of ground area (user-configurable setting)
- A '10 sq meter view' at 3cm GSD (typical drone survey) = ~333x333 pixels shown → tight enough to see the GCP target clearly

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:

1. PIPELINE CHANGE (gcp_pipeline.py / gcp_estimates.json):
   Add GSD (ground sample distance, cm/pixel) per image to estimates JSON.
   GSD can be computed from EXIF data already available in the pipeline:
     focal_mm, focal35_mm, img_w, img_h, agl (abs_alt - site_ellip_alt_m)
     GSD_cm = (sensor_width_mm / focal_mm) * agl_m * 100 / img_w
   Include in estimates JSON per image:
     { 'px': ..., 'py': ..., 'mode': ..., 'gsd_cm': 3.2 }

2. GCPEDITORPRO CHANGE (smartimage.component.ts):
   Add @Input() autoZoomSqM: number = 10 (configurable ground area in sq meters)
   Add @Input() gsdCm: number | null = null (from estimates sidecar)
   
   After image loads (img load event) and pinLocation is set:
   a. If gsdCm is available: compute target pixel extent
        side_px = sqrt(autoZoomSqM * 10000) / gsdCm   [10000 cm² per m²]
        scale = img_w / side_px  (approximate zoom level)
   b. Else (no GSD): fall back to showing ~400px around GCP (reasonable default)
   c. Call panzoom.zoom(scale) then pan to center pinLocation in the viewport
      panzoom has zoomToPoint(scale, point) which handles both in one call

3. SETTINGS:
   Add a configurable 'autoZoomSqM' setting (localStorage persisted) with default 10.
   Display in images-tagger toolbar or a settings panel.
   Set to 0 or null to disable auto-zoom (show full image).

NOTES:
- This applies to both Standard Grid View and Zoom View right panel
- If no pinLocation (no estimate): do not auto-zoom (show full image)
- panzoom.zoomToPoint(scale, focalPoint) is the key API call
  where focalPoint is in container-relative pixel coordinates
- The 250ms panzoom event debounce for pin placement is unrelated and unchanged
- GSD fallback (no EXIF data): use 400px default extent

PIPELINE SYNC:
After changing gcp_pipeline.py, sync to webodm/coreplugins/auto_gcp/pipeline.py
notes: GSD is already computable in the pipeline from EXIF (focal, sensor, agl). Add to estimates JSON. SmartimageComponent reads it and calls panzoom.zoomToPoint() on image load. Default 10 sq meters.
close_reason: Auto-zoom on load satisfied by _fitToContainer() which fits the image to the panel when autoFit=true. GSD-based zoom level and associated pipeline/JSON/UI changes dropped as unnecessary complexity.

description:

1. Remove `TOP_GCP_COUNT = 7` constant.

       OLD: hasPipelineEstimates && !isChk && gcpIndex < 7

   (CHK-* and DUP-* automatically excluded; works for any n_control value)

       count of GCP-* names in storage.gcps where confirmedCount >= CONFIRMED_GREEN

title: Merge rmse.py + rmse_visual_report.py into unified accuracy report tool
description:
Merge rmse.py and accuracy_study/rmse_visual_report.py into a single tool.

**Current state:** Two separate scripts — rmse.py (text+JSON) and rmse_visual_report.py (HTML with ortho crops). The report script calls rmse.py as a subprocess, parses JSON, then generates HTML. Redundant and fragile.

**Proposed unified CLI:**
  # Text + JSON only (backward compatible)
  python rmse.py recon.json gcp_list.txt chk_list.txt

  # HTML report without ortho crops
  python rmse.py recon.json gcp_list.txt chk_list.txt --html report.html

  # HTML report with annotated ortho crops
  python rmse.py recon.json gcp_list.txt chk_list.txt \
      --html report.html --ortho orthophoto.tif --targets targets.csv

**HTML structure:**
1. GCP residuals summary table (N, RMS_H, RMS_Z, RMS_3D)
2. CHK residuals summary table (same)
3. Per-point detail table (all points, sorted worst-first by dH):
   - label (bookmark link to image below), group, dH, dZ, d3D, suspect flag
4. Legend: green X = survey, yellow + = projected, yellow circle = 1ft
5. Annotated ortho crops (one per point, sorted worst-first)
   - Each has an anchor matching the table bookmark

**Annotations (per crop):**
- Green X: survey coordinate
- Yellow +: projected/triangulated position (survey + dX/dY)
- Yellow circle: 1ft radius centered on projected position

**Same script works with any ortho** — standard, DTM, true ortho. Run multiple times with different orthos for A/B comparison.

**Implementation:**
- Move HTML generation into rmse.py
- Delete accuracy_study/rmse_visual_report.py
- Keep text+JSON output on stderr/stdout (backward compat)
- --html flag triggers HTML generation
- --ortho + --targets enable crop images in HTML
close_reason: Merged: rmse.py now generates HTML report with --html flag. Tables (GCP/CHK summary + per-point detail sorted worst-first with bookmark links) + optional ortho crops (--ortho + --targets). Deleted accuracy_study/rmse_visual_report.py.

title: rmse_calc.py: two-file interface (gcp_list + chk_list)
description:
Update rmse_calc.py to accept two separate input files instead of one combined file.

## New CLI
    rmse_calc.py reconstruction.topocentric.json gcp_list.txt chk_list.txt

## Why two files are required
rmse_calc.py performs its own Umeyama similarity fit (7-parameter: scale, rotation, translation) from GCP triangulated positions → surveyed GCP positions. This corrects a real ~25m GPS translation offset and ~1.75° UTM grid convergence between reconstruction.topocentric.json's topocentric ENU frame and the surveyed EPSG:32613 coordinates. CHK points are independent (not used in OpenSFM BA), but they still need this similarity applied to produce valid residuals. Therefore rmse_calc.py always needs GCP rows for the similarity fit, even when the primary goal is CHK accuracy assessment.

## Internal logic changes
- Replace parse_combined(gcp_list_path) with two separate parse calls:
  parse_gcp(gcp_list_path) → crs_header, gcp_coords, gcp_obs
  parse_chk(chk_list_path) → chk_coords, chk_obs
- Both files have the same ODM tab-separated format; just different label prefixes
- CRS header taken from gcp_list.txt (as now)
- All downstream steps (triangulation, similarity fit, residuals, report) unchanged

## Use cases
- GCP accuracy (validation against ODM report):
    rmse_calc.py reconstruction.topocentric.json gcp_list.txt chk_list.txt
  → similarity fit from GCPs; GCP residuals should match ODM report
- CHK accuracy (independent assessment):
  same command; similarity applied to CHKs → true independent RMSE

## File
accuracy_study/rmse_calc.py
notes: geo-8bf confirmed: similarity fit correction is ~58m RMS_H without GCPs. Two-file interface is required — rmse_calc.py needs gcp_list.txt for the Umeyama similarity fit even when only CHK accuracy is the goal. Single-file chk_list.txt mode is NOT viable.
close_reason: Closed

title: docs: tagging guide — GCPs vs check points workflow
description:
Update docs/tagging-guide.md to explain the GCP vs check point distinction
and recommended workflow for a typical emlid2gcp.py survey.

## Sections to add / expand

### What are control points vs check points?
- Control (GCP-*): fed to ODM bundle adjustment; anchor the 3D model.
- Check (CHK-*): withheld from processing; used post-hoc to compute
  independent RMSE accuracy figures.
- Why the distinction matters: GCP residuals are optimistic (model fitted
  to them); CP residuals are the real accuracy metric.

### How accuracy (RMSE) is computed
- For each check point: dX, dY, dZ = model coordinate minus GNSS coordinate.
- RMSE_horizontal = sqrt(mean(dX²+dY²)); RMSE_vertical = sqrt(mean(dZ²)).
- Query DEM at check point GNSS location to get Z_model; query orthophoto for XY.
- Currently a manual step (QGIS or similar); ODM does not automate this yet.

### Recommended tagging workflow
Given a typical survey with ~78 surveyed points and pipeline top-7:

1. Tag top 7 GCP-* with ≥7 confirmed images each (pipeline order optimises
   spatial coverage + Z extremes + centroid anti-doming).
2. Tag 5–10 interior points as CHK-* with ≥3 confirmed images each.
   - Use the pipeline's sort order: points 8–15 are interior candidates.
   - Prefer points INSIDE the survey area, not at spatial extremes
     (extremes are already covered by GCPs).
   - Include points at varying elevations if terrain has significant relief.
3. Leave remaining ~65 points untagged (insurance against bad GCPs).
4. Do NOT use auto-refined (color_refined) estimates as check points without
   manual confirmation — pixel positions derived from the same imagery being
   processed are not fully independent.

### Exporting
- Download gcp_control.txt → feed to ODM as GCP input.
- Download gcp_check.txt  → use post-processing for RMSE calculation.
- Download gcp_list.txt   → reload / resume session.

### How many check points?
- Production mapping (ASPRS): ≥20.
- General drone survey: 5–10 is acceptable.
- Rule of thumb: ~10–15% of total surveyed points, distributed inside the
  survey area.

title: Implement GCP + image ordering in emlid2gcp.py
description:
Add structural priority sorting for GCPs and confidence sorting for images to the output of emlid2gcp.py, so that tagging in order produces optimal photogrammetric accuracy.

See docs/gcp-ordering.md for full algorithm spec.

## GCP sort (_sort_gcps)
1. Compute (mean_easting, mean_northing) centroid
2. Compute XY diagonal for scale reference
3. Z significance: z_range > z_threshold * xy_diagonal (default z_threshold=0.05)
4. Slot 1: argmax(dist_from_centroid)
5. Slot 2: argmax(dist_from_slot_1)
6. Slots 3+4: argmax/argmin(elevation) — only if Z significant
7. Next: argmin(dist_from_centroid) — center/anti-doming
8. Remaining: sorted by dist_from_centroid descending
Falls back to lat/lon/ellip_alt_m when projected coords unavailable.

## Z-critical GCP definition
Z-critical = Z is significant AND abs(elevation - mean_elev) / z_range > 0.80
These GCPs have obliques interleaved in their image ordering (see below).

## Image sort per GCP (_sort_images_for_gcp / _image_sort_score)
Sort key: score = normalized_dist + nadir_weight * (0 if nadir else 1)
- normalized_dist = sqrt((px-w/2)^2 + (py-h/2)^2) / (sqrt(w^2+h^2)/2)  [0-1]
- nadir_weight = 1.0 for normal GCPs (all nadir before oblique)
- nadir_weight = 0.3 for Z-critical GCPs (interleaves ~2-3 obliques in top 8)
- Reuses existing is_nadir() / NADIR_TOL_DEG
Falls back gracefully when img_w/img_h/gimbal_pitch absent.

## Changes required
- _sort_gcps(gcps, z_threshold) pure function — returns reordered list, tags z_critical GCPs
- _image_sort_score(px, py, img_w, img_h, gimbal_pitch, z_critical) pure function
- _sort_images_for_gcp(img_map, exif_map, z_critical) pure function
- _write_gcp_list() gains: sort_output, z_threshold, exif_map params
  Replace existing lexical/numeric sort (lines 535-539) with _sort_gcps
  Replace inner image iteration with _sort_images_for_gcp result
- run_pipeline() gains: sort_output=True, z_threshold=0.05 kwargs
  Print 'Sorting output for optimal accuracy with minimal tagging...' when sort_output=True
- CLI: --no-sort flag and --z-threshold float (default 0.05)
close_reason: Implemented and tested on ghostrider gulch (78 GCPs, 1738 images). GCP structural priority sort + per-GCP image confidence sort with z_critical interleaving. All three modes verified: default, --no-sort, --z-threshold.

title: Verify package.py-reprojected orthophoto aligns with survey points in QGIS
description:
After running package.py with --crs (via transform.yaml delivery_crs), visually
verify the reprojected orthophoto aligns correctly with known survey points in QGIS.

## Why critical
This is the first time package.py is performing CRS reprojection (via gdalwarp).
A silent misalignment (wrong source CRS assumed, axis order issue, etc.) would
produce a geometrically incorrect deliverable with no obvious error message.

## Steps
1. Open QGIS, set project CRS to EPSG:6529 (or EPSG:3618)
2. Load reprojected orthophoto output from package.py
3. Load F100340_AZTEC_points.csv (X=easting_ft, Y=northing_ft, CRS=EPSG:6529)
   or Emlid survey CSV (X=Easting, Y=Northing, CRS=EPSG:6529)
4. Confirm points fall on expected features in the orthophoto
5. Spot-check coordinates: E should be ~1,140,000–1,200,000 ft, N ~2,130,000–2,170,000 ft
   (NM Central state plane range for the Aztec site)

## Pass criteria
- Survey points land on their physical targets in the orthophoto
- No ~1 km or larger offset visible
- Coordinate display at click matches expected state-plane range

## Relevant files
- Input: ~/stratus/aztec3/odm_orthophoto/odm_orthophoto.original.tif (EPSG:32613)
- transform.yaml: ~/stratus/aztec4/transform.yaml (delivery_crs: EPSG:6529)
- Survey points: ~/stratus/aztec/F100340_AZTEC_points.csv
notes: Related to geo-7x1 (accuracy study) and geo-857/geo-51u (visual report + true ortho). The visual alignment verification is now primarily done via rmse_visual_report.py, not manual QGIS inspection. package.py CRS reprojection still needs verification but is secondary to ortho accuracy.

title: Implement CHK- role assignment in ODM GCP loader
description:
The custom ECR image has GroundControlPointRole C++ enum and hasattr guards in stats.py/io.py/reconstruction.py, but the gcp_list.txt parser never assigns role=METRICS_ONLY to CHK- prefixed rows. All points default to GCP role, so CHK points are used as control in BA and chk_errors in stats.json is always empty.

Fix: in ODM's GCP loader (where each gcp_list.txt row is parsed), add: if label.startswith('CHK-'): gcp.role = GroundControlPointRole.METRICS_ONLY

Evidence: aztec6 stats.json shows chk_errors={} with all 21 GCP+CHK points in gcp_errors with sub-mm residuals.
notes:
Full work required (beyond io.py guard):

1. io.py write-side hasattr guard (unblocks GCP loading with roles) — small change
2. Verify OpenSfM CHK residuals (computed in stats.py checkpoint_rmse_v2 code) flow correctly into ODM's stats reader
3. ODM report generator: populate chk_errors in stats.json from CHK residuals — currently the field exists but nothing writes to it
4. ODM report PDF template: add separate CHK accuracy section; remove CHK points from GCP table (currently all 21 appear there with sub-mm residuals)

Steps 3+4 are the bulk of the work — require changes to ODM's report generator and PDF template, not just OpenSfM.

Decision pending on whether to continue this path.

--- Root cause (from ~/stratus/aztec6/odm-bootstrap.log) ---
io.py _read_gcp_list_lines line 888: point.role = pymap.GroundControlPointRole.OPTIMIZATION
Crash: AttributeError: module 'opensfm.pymap' has no attribute 'GroundControlPointRole'
After crash: gcp=None, OpenSfM BA runs GPS-only. ODM georeferencing uses gcp_list_utm.txt externally.
chk_errors={} in stats.json, all 21 points in gcp_errors with 0.003m fit.
Role-assignment logic for GCP-/CHK- prefix IS in io.py — just needs write-side hasattr guard to unblock it.
close_reason: Design decision: keeping gcp_list.txt GCP-only is cleaner. CHK- role in ODM (METRICS_ONLY enum, hasattr guards, report changes) added significant fork complexity with no workflow gain — chk_list.txt feeds rmse_calc.py directly. Separate files are unambiguous and require no custom ODM image.

title: Research: point cloud ground classification — ODM DTM vs PDAL CSF vs CloudCompare vs WhiteboxTools
description:
Evaluate ground classification quality and usability across available tools using a real dataset (aztec6 or red-rocks once complete). Goal: find the best combination to replace Pix4D for survey-grade DTM/contour delivery.

## Tools to evaluate
- ODM --dtm (SMRF via PDAL, built-in) — baseline
- PDAL CSF filter (standalone, tunable)
- CloudCompare + CSF plugin (interactive, visual feedback)
- WhiteboxTools LidarGroundPointFilter / LidarSegmentationBasedFilter

## Evaluation criteria
- Classification quality on complex terrain (vegetation, structures, grade breaks)
- Tunable parameters — how much does tuning matter vs defaults?
- Automation potential (scriptable vs GUI-only)
- Learning curve for Isaiah

## Deliverables
- Side-by-side visual comparison of classified clouds
- GCP elevation residuals or known-flat-surface comparison for each method
- Short CloudCompare SOP (load LAS → run CSF → inspect → export) to give Isaiah a fair shot
- Recommendation: which tool(s) go into the production pipeline

## Notes
- CloudCompare was tried and abandoned by Isaiah due to learning curve — evaluate with a guide/SOP before dismissing
- PDAL CSF and CloudCompare CSF are the same algorithm; CloudCompare adds visual tuning feedback
- WhiteboxTools is open source and automatable — less known, worth a look
- LAStools (lasground) is best-in-class but commercial — probably not viable for production

title: Remove pagination nav from zoom view in images-tagger
description:
In images-tagger, the bottom pagination bar (ngb-pagination) is always rendered
regardless of view mode. In zoom view it is useless — all images are visible in
the scrollable left column, there is no paging — and clicking it does nothing
visible.

Fix: wrap the ngb-pagination element in *ngIf="!zoomView" so it only appears
in standard view. The 'Cancel' and 'Save Changes' buttons should remain visible
in both views.

File: src/app/images-tagger/images-tagger.component.html
The pagination is on the fixed-bottom bar, roughly:
  <ngb-pagination *ngIf="images.length > pageSize" ...>
Add the !zoomView condition:
  <ngb-pagination *ngIf="!zoomView && images.length > pageSize" ...>
close_reason: Fixed in GCPEditorPro commit e29a4aa

title: Spot vs on-demand cost analysis: interruption frequency, rework cost, break-even
description:
After a clean aztec6 run with interruption telemetry in place, produce a data-driven cost model for spot vs on-demand for m5.4xlarge and g4dn.12xlarge (once quota approved).

## Data sources needed
- aws ec2 describe-spot-price-history for m5.4xlarge us-west-2 (last 30 days) — price variance
- AWS Spot Advisor: current interruption frequency rating for m5.4xlarge us-west-2
- Grafana timeline: gap durations from interruptions, which stage was running, recovery time
- ODM stage timing from Grafana annotations (per-stage duration baseline)

## Analysis to produce
1. Actual spot price vs on-demand for current and target instance types
2. Interruption frequency (observed + AWS Advisor rating)
3. Per-interruption rework cost: which stage was interrupted × stage duration
4. Break-even: how many interruptions per run before spot loses its advantage
5. Recommendation: spot as default? On-demand for specific conditions (e.g. GPU jobs)?

## Instance types to cover
- m5.4xlarge: current workhorse (~/bin/zsh.768/hr on-demand, ~/bin/zsh.15-0.25/hr spot)
- g4dn.12xlarge: pending quota (~.912/hr on-demand, ~.17-1.96/hr spot)

## Notes
- 4 interruptions last night was unusually high — likely capacity spike, not representative
- Depends on spot interruption telemetry bead being in place first (geo-XXX)
- ODM feature extraction checkpoints per-image; bundle adjustment does not — interruption during reconstruction is worst-case rework
- Goal: decide whether spot is the right default long-term, and whether policy differs for GPU vs CPU instances

title: Implement CRS-aware filenames and multi-CRS outputs across pipeline
description:
Implement the pipeline changes reflected in docs/odm-workflow.md.
Reference that diagram as the authoritative spec throughout.

## Naming convention
No CRS suffix = EPSG:32613 (the default/Cloud CRS).
Suffix required when NOT 32613: _6529, _design.

## transform.py dc — new outputs
Currently writes: {job}_points.csv (state-plane, EPSG:6529)
After: write two files (no EPSG:32613 output — covered by sight.py + transform.py split):
  {job}_points_6529.csv   — state-plane ft (current content, renamed; for Emlid localization)
  {job}_points_design.csv — design-grid ft (new; apply shift_x/shift_y; for QGIS design review)
transform.yaml unchanged.
Emlid input filenames (produced by Emlid RS3) renamed per convention:
  {job}_surveyed_6529.csv (was {job}_all.csv)
NOTE: {job}_6529.csv (the manually-filtered version) is eliminated — filtering
is now done by sight.py --filter. These are user-managed files; update
docs/odm-workflow.md step-by-step prose and any grep references.

## sight.py — filter + reproject output to EPSG:32613
Currently: outputs {job}.txt with EPSG:6529 header; manual filter step precedes it
After:
  --filter <string>  whole-row substring match against raw CSV lines
    Matching rows     → {job}.txt (EPSG:32613; reproject via pyproj field_crs→odm_crs)
    Non-matching rows → {job}_other.csv (EPSG:32613; customer monuments for QGIS review)
  marks_design.csv: apply design_grid shift to produce design-grid coords (new behavior)
Requires pyproj (already available in geo conda env).
Removes need for manual filter step and {job}_6529.csv file.

## transform.py split — add design-grid output + simplify 32613 path
Currently: reads EPSG:6529 confirmed.txt, reprojects to EPSG:32613
After: reads EPSG:32613 {job}_tagged.txt (no reprojection needed for gcp/chk),
also writes a combined design-grid file by applying shift from transform.yaml design_grid:
  gcp_list.txt          — EPSG:32613, GCP- points only (current content)
  chk_list.txt          — EPSG:32613, CHK- points only (current content)
  {job}_tagged_design.txt — design-grid ft, all tagged points (new; replaces separate gcp/chk design files)
Input filename change: confirmed.txt → {job}_tagged.txt (matches Isaiah's terminology)

## EC2 / Terraform — verify gcp_list.txt reference
infra/ec2/scripts/odm-run.sh references gcp_list.txt — filename unchanged, but verify.

## Testing
Test dir: ~/stratus/aztec5/ (contains .dc file, Emlid CSV, tagged.txt, transformed ortho)
Ground truth: ~/stratus/ghostrider gulch/gcp_confirmed.txt
Run: conda run -n geo python compare_refinement.py ...
Verify QGIS alignment:
  Cloud review:  load {job}_other.csv + gcp_list.txt + chk_list.txt + odm_orthophoto (all EPSG:32613)
  Design review: load {job}_points_design.csv + {job}_tagged_design.txt + delivered ortho

## Key files to change
- transform.py  (dc and split subcommands)
- TargetSighter/sight.py
- docs/odm-workflow.md step-by-step prose (Steps 1-4)
- docs/coordinate-flow.md (all filename references)
close_reason: implemented: transform.py dc writes _6529 + _design CSVs; sight.py --filter splits matching/other rows, reprojects to EPSG:32613; transform.py split reads file header CRS (authoritative), writes tagged_design.txt; marks.csv renamed marks_design.csv

title: sight.py: use calibrated Brown model from previous ODM run for better initial GCP projections
description:
The Brown camera model (focal_x/y, c_x/c_y, k1/k2/k3/p1/p2) represents physical lens properties — highly stable across sites for the same camera. A calibrated model from a previous ODM run should be reusable with minimal site-to-site variation (fixed-focal drone always at infinity focus; temperature effects <0.01%).

**Problem with current sight.py**: uses EXIF focal length + assumed principal point, no distortion correction. Radial distortion (k1/k2) shifts pixels 10–30px at image edges — this is where GCPs often appear. A calibrated Brown model would improve initial pixel projections and reduce manual adjustment time in GCPEditorPro.

**Implementation**:
- Accept optional --cameras cameras.json argument in sight.py
- Parse Brown model: focal_x, focal_y, c_x, c_y, k1, k2, k3, p1, p2 (from reconstruction.json or cameras.json format)
- Apply full Brown distortion in project_3d_to_pixel() instead of simple pinhole
- Camera key format: 'v2 <make> <model> <width> <height> brown' — must match drone

**Workflow**:
- After any ODM run: pull cameras.json from S3 (e.g. ~/stratus/cameras_dji_fc6310r.json)
- Pass to sight.py: --cameras ~/stratus/cameras_dji_fc6310r.json
- Per-job cameras.json lives at s3://stratus-jrstear/{PROJECT}/cameras.json after ODM dataset stage
close_reason: implemented and tested: Brown model improves both pixel placement and image set (162 new images via corrected bounds check) and sort order (more accurate distance-from-center ranking)

title: GCPEditorPro: spacebar confirms current GCP estimate in zoom view
description:
## Goal
Speed up confirmation of color-refined estimates: instead of clicking each
sub-image, the user can hover over a crop thumbnail and press Space to
confirm the estimated position in-place, then move to the next image.

## Behaviour
- Only active in zoom view (`this.zoomView === true`).
- Pressing Space while any crop thumbnail is hovered (i.e. `selectedDesc` is
  set) and `selectedDesc.isTagged` is true: confirms the estimate at its
  current pixel position (keeps `imX`/`imY` unchanged, sets `confirmed=true`
  and `confidence="confirmed"`).
- After confirming, advances `selectedDesc` to the next unconfirmed-tagged
  image in `this.images`, wrapping to the first image if none remain.
- If `selectedDesc` is null or not tagged, Space is a no-op (no error).
- Space must not fire if focus is inside a text input, number input, or
  any other form field (check `e.target.tagName`).

## Implementation — images-tagger.component.ts
- Add `@HostListener("window:keydown", ["$event"])` handler `onKeyDown(e: KeyboardEvent)`.
- Guard: `if (e.code \!== "Space") return;`
- Guard: `if (\!this.zoomView || \!this.selectedDesc?.isTagged) return;`
- Guard: `if (["INPUT","TEXTAREA","SELECT"].includes((e.target as HTMLElement).tagName)) return;`
- Call `this.pin({ x: this.selectedDesc.image.imX, y: this.selectedDesc.image.imY }, this.selectedDesc)`.
- Advance: find next image after selectedDesc in `this.images` that is not yet
  confirmed; fall back to first unconfirmed; if all confirmed keep selection.
- Call `e.preventDefault()` to prevent page scroll on Space.

## No other files need changes
- `pin()` already sets confirmed=true and confidence="confirmed" (after geo-0cu).
- No HTML changes needed.

## UX intent
The crop column in zoom view shows images in confidence order. For a well-refined
GCP, the user can work down the column rapidly: glance at crop, hover, Space,
repeat — without ever reaching for the mouse to click a precise pixel.
close_reason: Closed
